Typical workflow: 1) Enable it: git config --global rerere.enabled true 2) Do a rebase that conflicts. 3) Resolve conflicts and continue. Git records your resolutions in .git/rr-cache. Later, when rebasing again, Git may say “Resolved using previous resolution.” Caveat: It’s not magic; if conflicts differ significantly, it won’t apply. Also you should still verify the result, because automatic resolution might be wrong if context changed.
You mentioned interactive rebase for cleaning up commits. Can you walk me through a realistic example?

Sure. Suppose your branch has these commits (newest on top): pick a1b2c3 Add parser skeleton pick d4e5f6 WIP: debug prints pick 112233 Fix tests pick 445566 Oops, fix typo pick 778899 Refactor parser to handle edge cases pick abcdef Address review: rename variable You want to: - squash “WIP debug prints” into the relevant commit or drop it, - squash typo fix into the commit that introduced it, - maybe reorder so refactor happens after skeleton and before test fixes, - and edit commit messages. You run: git rebase -i HEAD~6 An editor opens: pick a1b2c3 Add parser skeleton pick d4e5f6 WIP: debug prints pick 112233 Fix tests pick 445566 Oops, fix typo pick 778899 Refactor parser to handle edge cases pick abcdef Address review: rename variable You might change it to: pick a1b2c3 Add parser skeleton pick 778899 Refactor parser to handle edge cases pick 112233 Fix tests fixup 445566 Oops, fix typo reword abcdef Rename variable for clarity drop d4e5f6 WIP: debug prints Or maybe “debug prints” should be removed entirely; if the final code doesn’t include them, dropping is fine. If it introduced something later undone, you can drop or squash depending on your preference. Commands: - pick: use commit as-is - reword: keep content but edit message - edit: stop and let you amend - squash: combine commit with previous, keep both messages (you can edit combined message) - fixup: combine with previous, discard this commit message - drop: remove commit entirely After saving, Git replays commits in that order, rewriting history.
If I drop a commit during interactive rebase, is it gone forever?

Not immediately. Git tends to keep unreachable commits around for a while, and you can recover them via reflog. If you dropped commit d4e5f6, it becomes unreachable from branch pointers. But you can often find it: git reflog Look for the old HEAD positions before the rebase. Then you can create a branch pointing to it: git branch rescue d4e5f6 Or if you don’t remember the hash, you can use: git fsck --lost-found But reflog is usually enough. Eventually, unreachable commits can be garbage-collected (git gc), but that’s not immediate.
I see. Another confusion: “merge creates a merge commit.” But sometimes merge doesn’t create one. Why?

Because merge has two modes depending on whether it can fast-forward. - If the target branch tip is an ancestor of the branch being merged, Git can fast-forward, meaning it just moves the pointer—no new commit. - If histories diverged, Git must create a merge commit to combine two parents (unless you do something like rebase first, or use a different strategy). You can control it: - --no-ff: force a merge commit even if a fast-forward is possible. - --ff-only: refuse to merge unless it can fast-forward. Example: git merge --ff-only feature This will fail if main has diverged, which can be useful if you want to avoid merge commits in that situation.
What about “squash merge”? Is that a merge or a rebase?

Squash merge is kind of a third thing: it takes the net changes introduced by a branch and applies them as a single new commit on the target branch, without preserving the individual commits from the branch. Command-line: git merge --squash feature git commit This does not create a merge commit with two parents; it creates a normal commit with one parent (the previous tip of the target branch). It also does not move the feature branch pointer. So: - It’s not rebase (no replay of each commit; it’s one combined diff). - It’s not a true merge commit either (no two-parent commit). It’s useful if you want: - a single commit on main for the whole feature, - but you don’t care about preserving the feature branch’s internal commit history on main. Downside: you lose per-commit history when looking at main. Also Git can’t automatically know that the branch was integrated (since there’s no merge relationship), so future merges may behave differently unless you delete the branch or manage carefully.
If I do squash merge and later merge the same branch normally, will it reapply changes?

It can, because Git uses commit ancestry to decide what’s already merged. With squash merge, the original commits from the feature branch are not ancestors of main, so Git doesn’t see them as merged. If you later try to merge feature, Git may attempt to reintroduce changes, potentially causing conflicts or duplicate changes. There are ways around it (like deleting the feature branch after squash, or using strategies), but generally: after squash merging, you typically don’t merge that branch again; you consider it “done.” If you must keep working, you can: - reset the feature branch to the new main tip after squash, or - create a new branch from main and continue.
Is cherry-pick closer to rebase?

Cherry-pick is closer to rebase in the sense that it also creates new commits by applying changes from existing commits, but it’s selective and not necessarily contiguous. - git cherry-pick <commit> applies the diff introduced by that commit onto your current HEAD, creating a new commit with a new hash. - git rebase replays a sequence of commits (often a contiguous range) onto a new base. Both rewrite by copying commits, but rebase preserves the order and tries to move a whole branch. Cherry-pick is for “I want that change, not the whole branch.” Cherry-pick is great for backporting a fix or pulling a specific change. But it can lead to duplicate commits across branches if overused, making history harder to reason about.
Sometimes after rebase, I see “skipping commit” messages. What does that mean?

During rebase, Git may detect that a commit being replayed is effectively already present in the new base (often via “patch-id” comparison). In that case it can skip applying it to avoid duplicates. This can happen when: - you previously cherry-picked a commit from your branch into the base branch, - you previously rebased and some commits are duplicates, - the same change exists with a different hash. Git compares the patch (diff) rather than commit hash, so it can say “this patch is already in upstream,” and skip it. If you want to force it to apply anyway (rarely needed), there are options, but usually skipping is correct. Still, it’s worth checking that the resulting code includes what you expect.
How does patch-id work? Is it reliable?

Patch-id is a way to identify a change by its diff content rather than commit hash. Git computes a hash of a normalized diff (ignoring some metadata like whitespace in certain contexts) to see if two commits represent the same change. It’s “reliable enough” for detecting duplicate changes in many cases, but not perfect: - If the same logical change is implemented differently (different diff), patch-id differs. - Context changes can alter the diff even if the end result is similar. - Renames, file mode changes, or conflict resolutions can alter patch-id. So it’s a heuristic to avoid duplicates, not a formal proof.
Can you explain with an example when merge is better than rebase even if I want clean history?

One example is when the fact of integration matters more than linearity. Suppose you have two long-running branches that represent different conceptual efforts, and you want to preserve the record of when they were combined because debugging later might depend on that boundary. A merge commit acts as a marker: “these two lines met here.” Also, if you have merges within your branch (like you merged a side branch into your feature), a plain rebase flattens that structure by default. You can preserve it with --rebase-merges, but it’s more complex. Another case: if you’ve already published commits and you value stable commit IDs (e.g., references in documentation, bug reports, or external systems). Merge keeps IDs stable. So “clean history” doesn’t necessarily mean “linear.” A history can be clean but non-linear if merge commits are meaningful and messages are good.
What exactly is --rebase-merges? I’ve never used it.

By default, rebase takes the commits in your branch and replays them as a linear sequence, ignoring merge commits (it will usually flatten them). --rebase-merges tells Git to try to preserve the branch topology by recreating merge commits during the rebase. Example: your branch has a merge commit because you merged a sub-branch: main: A—B—C \ feature:   D—E—M—F / X—Y   (sub-branch merged into feature at M) A normal rebase of feature onto new main would flatten X,Y into the linear series (or sometimes drop the merge structure). With --rebase-merges, Git tries to replay D,E, then recreate the merge of X,Y, then continue with F, resulting in a similar shape but with new commit IDs. It’s useful if the merge structure is meaningful (e.g., you intentionally merged two sets of changes and want to keep that grouping). It can be more conflict-prone and more complex to resolve, but it’s sometimes the right tool.
I often do “git pull” and it sometimes merges, sometimes rebases. What’s happening there?

git pull is essentially two commands: 1) git fetch 2) then either git merge (default) or git rebase (if configured or requested) So: - Default: git pull = fetch + merge FETCH_HEAD into current branch - With --rebase: git pull --rebase = fetch + rebase current branch onto fetched upstream If you see merges after pull, it’s because your local branch had commits not on the remote, and a merge was performed to combine them. If you want pull to rebase by default (to keep your local branch linear), you can set: git config --global pull.rebase true Or per-branch: git config branch.<name>.rebase true There’s also: git config --global pull.ff only which makes pull refuse if it would create a merge commit (only fast-forward). This is stricter: it forces you to decide how to reconcile divergence.
If I set pull.rebase true, can that be dangerous?

It can be surprising if you’re not expecting it, but it’s not inherently dangerous if you understand the implications. What it does: when you pull and you have local commits, Git will rebase those local commits on top of the fetched upstream, rewriting your local commits (new hashes). If those local commits were already pushed somewhere else, you can end up with divergence and need force pushes. So it’s “safe” when: - your local commits are truly local (not pushed), - or you’re comfortable force-pushing your branch and you know nothing else depends on those old hashes. A safer variant is: git pull --rebase=merges or configure rebase options, but the core issue remains: rebasing rewrites. If you’re frequently making local commits and then pulling updates, pull --rebase can reduce merge commits and keep things tidy.
Sometimes I see “fatal: Need to specify how to reconcile divergent branches.” What causes that?

That message appears in newer Git versions when you run git pull and Git detects divergence (both local and remote have commits the other doesn’t), but you haven’t configured whether pull should merge, rebase, or fast-forward only. You fix it by choosing a policy: - Merge (default historically): git config --global pull.rebase false - Rebase: git config --global pull.rebase true - Fast-forward only: git config --global pull.ff only Or specify per invocation: git pull --rebase git pull --no-rebase git pull --ff-only Git is forcing explicitness to avoid accidental merges or rebases.
How do I decide between pull.ff only and pull.rebase true?

pull.ff only is strict: it refuses if your local branch isn’t a direct ancestor of the remote branch. That means you must manually decide what to do whenever you have local commits and remote has new commits. - Pros: no surprise merges or rebases; you can’t accidentally create a merge commit or rewrite commits via pull. - Cons: more manual steps; you’ll hit errors more often if you commit locally before pulling. pull.rebase true is more automatic: - Pros: keeps local history linear; avoids merge commits during pull; fewer interruptions. - Cons: rewrites your local commits; if those commits were pushed elsewhere, you’ll need to handle that carefully. If you prefer explicit control and don’t mind extra steps, ff-only is great. If you prefer a smoother “keep up to date” flow and are careful about rebasing only local commits, pull.rebase is convenient.
Can we do a step-by-step example with actual commands? Like I have main and feature, main moved, feature moved, and I want to integrate.

Sure. Let’s simulate: Start: main: A—B—C feature: branched from C You add commits on feature: feature: C—D—E Meanwhile main gets commits: main: A—B—C—F—G Now feature is behind main and also ahead. Option 1: Merge main into feature (to update feature), then merge feature into main. Commands: 1) Update your local main: git checkout main git pull (or fetch + merge/ff) 2) Switch to feature: git checkout feature 3) Merge main into feature: git merge main If conflicts, resolve, then commit merge commit M (Git will create it). Now feature looks like: C—D—E—M \ / F—G (from main) 4) Then when ready, merge feature into main: git checkout main git merge feature This merge might be fast-forward or might create another merge commit depending on whether main moved again. Usually it creates a merge commit if there’s divergence. Option 2: Rebase feature onto main, then fast-forward merge into main. Commands: 1) Update main: git checkout main git pull 2) Rebase feature: git checkout feature git rebase main Resolve conflicts per commit, then continue: git rebase --continue Now feature is: A—B—C—F—G—D'—E' 3) Integrate into main by fast-forward: git checkout main git merge --ff-only feature Now main is linear: A—B—C—F—G—D'—E' Note: D and E became D' and E'. That’s the rewrite.
In option 2, why do we fast-forward merge after rebase instead of just “git rebase main” while on main?

Because rebase moves commits from one base to another; it’s typically used on the branch that contains the commits you want to transplant. If you’re on main and you run git rebase feature, you’re rebasing main onto feature, which is usually the opposite of what you want: it would try to replay main’s commits on top of feature. That can change main’s history, which is generally riskier. The typical approach: - Rebase the topic branch onto main (rewrite the topic branch) - Then integrate by fast-forwarding main to the topic branch tip (no rewrite to main, just moving pointer forward) So main’s history stays stable (except it advances), and only feature’s commits are rewritten.
What if I accidentally rebased main? Can I undo it?

Often yes, via reflog. If you rebased main and want to return to the previous state: 1) Check reflog: git reflog main or git reflog You’ll see entries like: <hash1> main@{0}: rebase finished: returning to refs/heads/main <hash2> main@{1}: rebase: ... Find the commit main pointed to before the rebase (say <oldhash>). 2) Reset main back: git checkout main git reset --hard <oldhash> If you had already pushed the rebased main, undoing becomes more complex because you’d need to push the reset (force) and consider any downstream effects. But locally, reflog makes it recoverable.
I’ve seen “git merge-base” mentioned. Is that useful for understanding rebase/merge?

Yes. git merge-base finds the best common ancestor(s) of two commits—often “where the branches diverged.” Example: git merge-base main feature It returns the hash of the commit that is the common ancestor. That’s effectively the “old base” used in many operations. For rebase, the commits to replay are basically: (feature tip) minus (merge-base(main, feature)) For merge, the merge base is used to compute a three-way merge: base vs ours vs theirs. So merge-base is a good diagnostic tool: - If Git is choosing an unexpected base during rebase, check merge-base. - If conflicts seem weird, understanding the base can help.
Three-way merge is another term I’ve heard. Can you explain it simply?

A three-way merge combines changes from two branches using a common ancestor as the reference point. You have: - base: the merge base (common ancestor) - ours: the current branch tip - theirs: the branch you’re merging in Git computes diffs: - base → ours - base → theirs Then tries to apply both sets of changes to the base. If both changed different parts, it merges cleanly. If both changed the same lines differently, conflict. This is why merges can succeed even if both branches changed the same file: Git looks at what changed relative to the base. Rebase also uses merge machinery under the hood when applying commits, but it does it commit-by-commit.
Is there a performance difference? Like is rebase slower?

Usually not in a way that matters for typical repo sizes, but there are differences: - Merge: one merge operation (though it may be complex). - Rebase: potentially many cherry-pick-like applications (one per commit), so if you have a large number of commits, rebase can take longer and can require more conflict resolutions. But Git is quite fast; the main “cost” is human time resolving conflicts, not CPU time. For huge histories or large binaries, either can be slower, but it’s rarely the deciding factor.
I sometimes do “git pull --rebase” and then realize I wanted to keep my local merge commits. Can I rebase but keep merges?

Yes: git pull --rebase=merges (or git rebase --rebase-merges) tries to preserve merge commits. However, preserving merges while rebasing can be tricky. It recreates merges as new merge commits with new hashes, so it still rewrites history, just with topology preserved. If you already did a plain rebase and want to undo and redo with merges preserved: - Use reflog to go back to before the rebase - Then run rebase with --rebase-merges Example: git reflog git reset --hard HEAD@{1}   (or whichever entry is pre-rebase) git rebase --rebase-merges origin/main
Another thing: How does “git log” show merges vs rebases? Any tips for visualizing?

For visualization, these are useful: - Graph view: git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all - Show first-parent history (useful to follow mainline through merges): git log --first-parent --oneline - Use --simplify-by-decoration to see branch points and tags: git log --graph --decorate --oneline --simplify-by-decoration If you merge, you’ll see merge commits with two parents and the graph will show branches converging. If you rebase and then fast-forward, the graph is mostly a straight line. Also: git show <merge_commit> will show the merge commit’s parents and the combined diff.
You said “first-parent” helps. What does that mean?

In a merge commit, there are two (or more) parents. “First parent” is the parent that represents the branch you were on when you created the merge commit (the “ours” side). Using --first-parent on main shows the mainline progression ignoring the details of merged-in branches. It’s a way to read history as: “what commits landed on main, and what merges happened,” without diving into every commit in merged branches. This is especially useful in merge-heavy histories: it makes log readable.
If I rebase, do I lose the ability to see when something was merged?

You lose merge commits (unless you preserve them with --rebase-merges), so you lose explicit integration markers. But you can still infer some things via commit dates, though those can be misleading because rebase changes committer dates (author date stays the same by default, committer date becomes “now”). So if “when it was integrated” matters, merge commits are better evidence. Also note: - Author date: when the commit was originally authored. - Committer date: when the commit was last committed (rebases update this). git log by default shows author date, but you can configure formats.
Wait, rebase changes committer date but not author date?

By default, yes. When you rebase, Git creates new commits. It typically preserves the original author identity and author timestamp, but the committer identity and committer timestamp become the person/time doing the rebase. You can see both with: git show --format=fuller <commit> That will show: AuthorDate: CommitDate: This distinction is useful when you want to know when a change was written vs when it was integrated/recreated.
If I want to keep the original commit date even after rebase, can I?

You can preserve author dates (default) but committer date will still change because it’s a new commit. There is an option: git rebase --committer-date-is-author-date which sets the committer date to match the author date for rebased commits. Some people like it for consistent timelines; others prefer committer date to reflect the rebase time. There’s also: git rebase --ignore-date which sets author date to current time (usually not desired). In general, I’d leave defaults unless you have a specific reason.
How does this relate to “git blame”? People say rebase messes up blame.

Rebasing changes commit hashes, but git blame is about line history, not hash stability. In many cases, blame will still show the correct author and original author date, since those are preserved. However, rebase can affect blame in a few ways: - If you squash commits, you change which commit is credited for lines (now the squashed commit). - If you reorder or edit commits, the “last commit touching this line” might change. - If you do a large reformat, blame becomes noisy regardless of rebase vs merge. So the real “blame disruption” is more about squashing, fixup, and changing commit structure than rebase itself. Rebase without squashing (just moving base) typically preserves author metadata, but commit IDs change, which matters if someone referenced a specific hash in discussions.
Is there a way to rebase without changing commit hashes?

No. Changing the parent changes the commit content, which changes the hash. Git commit IDs are content-addressed. Any change to parent, message, timestamps, tree, etc. yields a different hash. If you want to integrate without changing existing commits, use merge.
Let’s talk about conflicts again. Is there a strategy to minimize conflicts when rebasing?

Yes—several: 1) Rebase frequently (small deltas) If you rebase your branch onto main regularly, each rebase is smaller and conflicts are easier. 2) Keep commits focused If a commit mixes unrelated changes, conflicts become harder. Interactive rebase can help you split commits: git rebase -i mark a commit as edit, then: git reset HEAD^ git add -p git commit repeat to split. 3) Use rerere As mentioned, it can auto-apply repeated resolutions. 4) Prefer “mechanical refactors” isolated from logic changes If you plan to rename symbols or reformat, do it in separate commits. It reduces conflict overlap. 5) Use conflict style and mergetool Set conflict style to show base: git config --global merge.conflictStyle zdiff3 (or diff3) Then conflicts show base/ours/theirs, often clarifying intent.
I didn’t know about merge.conflictStyle. What’s diff3 and zdiff3?

merge.conflictStyle controls how conflict markers are presented. - default: shows ours and theirs - diff3: shows base as well, with an extra section marked by ||||||| for the common ancestor - zdiff3: a newer style that tries to show a more compact/clear base section (depending on Git version) Example conflict with diff3: <<<<<<< ours line from ours ||||||| base line from base ======= line from theirs >>>>>>> theirs Seeing base helps you understand what changed on each side.
If I rebase and decide it’s too messy, can I abort?

Yes. During a rebase in progress: - Abort and return to the original state: git rebase --abort - If you resolved conflicts and want to continue: git rebase --continue - If you want to skip the current commit (not apply it): git rebase --skip Abort is safe as long as you haven’t done additional destructive operations. It restores branch to the state before the rebase started.
What about merge? Can I abort a merge?

Yes, if the merge is in progress and uncommitted: - If you started a merge and got conflicts: git merge --abort This tries to return to the pre-merge state. If for some reason that fails (rare), you can do: git reset --hard HEAD but be careful: that discards working tree changes. If you already committed the merge, “aborting” means reverting or resetting: - git reset --hard <pre-merge-hash> (rewrites history) - or git revert -m 1 <merge_commit_hash> (creates a new commit that undoes the merge; keeps history)
Reverting a merge commit always confuses me. What does -m do?

A merge commit has two parents. Reverting it requires choosing which parent to treat as the “mainline,” because the merge commit represents combining both histories. git revert -m <parent-number> <merge_commit> - -m 1 means “keep the first parent’s content as mainline, and revert the changes introduced by merging the second parent.” - -m 2 means the opposite. Usually, when you merged a branch into main, the first parent is main, so you use -m 1 to revert the merge while keeping main’s side. It’s confusing because reverting a merge doesn’t “undo history,” it creates a new commit that introduces an inverse change relative to the chosen mainline.
Okay. Switching gears: Is there a difference between “git rebase” and “git pull --rebase”?

git pull --rebase is just combining fetch + rebase in one step, and it rebases your current branch onto its upstream tracking branch (typically origin/<branch>). git rebase is more general: you can rebase onto any commit/branch and you can specify ranges. So: - git pull --rebase: update from remote and rebase local commits on top - git rebase <branch>: rebase current branch onto <branch> using local refs (you may need fetch first) If you want explicit control, you can do: git fetch git rebase origin/main That’s often clearer because you decide exactly what you’re rebasing onto.
How do tracking branches factor in? Like origin/main vs main.

origin/main is a remote-tracking branch—your local record of what the remote’s main looked like the last time you fetched. main is your local branch. When you fetch: - origin/main updates to match the remote. - main doesn’t move unless you merge/rebase/reset it. So it’s common to rebase feature onto origin/main (after fetch) because that’s the freshest view of the upstream state: git fetch origin git checkout feature git rebase origin/main Using main is fine if your local main is already up to date, but using origin/main avoids relying on that assumption.
I sometimes get confused which branch I’m on. Any tips to avoid messing up rebase/merge?

A few practical habits: - Always check: git status It tells you the current branch and whether you’re in the middle of a rebase/merge. - Use: git branch --show-current - Use prompt integration (shows branch name and rebase state), if you have it. - Before rebasing/merging, look at: git log --oneline --decorate -n 10 - For safety, create a temporary backup branch before risky history edits: git branch backup-feature Then if you mess up, you can reset to backup-feature.
You mentioned splitting commits earlier. Can you show the exact steps?

Sure. Suppose commit E contains two unrelated changes and you want to split it into E1 and E2. 1) Start interactive rebase to stop at that commit: git rebase -i <base> In the todo list, change “pick <E>” to “edit <E>”. 2) When Git stops at E: git reset HEAD^ This “uncommits” E but keeps its changes in your working tree (mixed reset). Now the changes are unstaged. 3) Stage part of the changes: git add -p (choose hunks interactively) 4) Commit the first part: git commit -m "E1 message" 5) Stage the remaining changes: git add -p (or git add . if everything left belongs together) 6) Commit the second part: git commit -m "E2 message" 7) Continue rebase: git rebase --continue Git will then replay subsequent commits on top of your two new commits.
If I do git reset HEAD^ during rebase, does it mess up the rebase state?

It’s a standard technique and doesn’t break rebase as long as you continue properly. During an “edit” stop, rebase expects you to amend or replace the commit at HEAD. Resetting HEAD^ is just a way to unpack that commit. The main caution: don’t run commands that move branch pointers unexpectedly outside the rebase context (like switching branches) unless you know what you’re doing. But reset and recommit is fine.
Another topic: Is “rebase” the same as “changing base branch” in hosting UIs?

Often, when a UI says “change base branch” or “update branch,” it might do one of: - merge the base into your branch (creating a merge commit), - rebase your branch onto base (rewriting), - or just change the comparison target in the UI (no Git history change). So you can’t assume. In Git terms, “changing base” can mean rebasing, but it could also mean merging base into the branch. You’d need to check what operation it actually performs or what your local history looks like afterward.
I’m trying to form a personal rule: “always rebase feature branches before merging.” Is that a good rule?

It can be a good rule if you interpret it carefully: - Rebase your feature branch onto the latest main before integrating, to ensure it applies cleanly and to keep history linear. - But avoid rebasing commits that are already published in a way you care about preserving (because you’ll force-push). If your rule includes: - “rebase only my own branch commits,” - “use --force-with-lease if I already pushed,” - “don’t rebase main,” then it’s a workable approach. But if you find you often need the integration context or prefer stable commit IDs, a merge-based approach might be better. A more nuanced personal rule: - Use rebase to keep your branch up to date (locally) and to clean up commits (interactive rebase). - Use merge when you want to preserve the exact integration event or avoid rewriting.
What about “merge commits are evil” vs “rebasing is evil”? Why are opinions so strong?

Because both can be misused, and different people optimize for different failure modes. Anti-merge-commit sentiment often comes from: - messy histories with lots of “Merge branch …” noise from frequent merging, - hard-to-read logs, - bisect jumping through merge commits. Anti-rebase sentiment often comes from: - broken references due to rewritten hashes, - confusion when history changes under you, - force-push mistakes overwriting commits, - difficulty correlating external references to commits. Neither is inherently evil. The strong opinions are usually a reaction to pain from one style used poorly.
How does bisect behave with merge commits?

git bisect works on commits; with merge commits, the graph isn’t linear. Git still can bisect, but the presence of merges can complicate the search because a merge commit combines two lines. Sometimes a bug is introduced on one side branch but not the other; the merge commit is where it becomes reachable from main. Bisect may land on a merge commit, and determining “good” or “bad” might not be straightforward because the merge commit includes both parents’ changes. You can: - Mark the merge commit as bad and continue; Git will choose another commit. - Or use first-parent logs for analysis. - Some people prefer linear history (rebases or fast-forward merges) to simplify bisect. Still, merge commits aren’t incompatible with bisect; they just add complexity.
Is there a way to avoid merge commits but also avoid rebasing?

You can avoid merge commits by requiring fast-forward merges only. That means you must ensure your branch is always up to date and can be fast-forwarded—typically by rebasing (or by resetting and re-applying changes) when main moves. So in practice, “no merge commits ever” often implies some form of rebasing or cherry-picking/squashing, because you need linearization. If you want to avoid both merge commits and rebasing, the only way is: never diverge—i.e., don’t have parallel branches. But that’s not realistic for most workflows.
Suppose I made a feature branch, pushed it, then someone else (or another device) pulled it, and I rebase and force-push. What happens to the other clone?

The other clone will have the old commits and its local branch pointer might still point to the old tip. After it fetches, it will see the remote branch moved to new commits (rewritten history). The old commits are no longer on the remote branch. To reconcile, that clone can: - reset its local branch to the new remote tip (discarding local divergence), or - rebase its local work onto the new tip, or - merge, etc. If that clone had no additional local commits, easiest is: git fetch git checkout feature git reset --hard origin/feature If it had local commits based on the old history, it may need to rebase them onto the new branch tip. This is why rebasing published branches requires coordination and care—even across your own machines.
Let’s talk about “upstream” in rebase. What is upstream exactly?

In Git, a local branch can have an “upstream” (tracking) branch, typically a remote-tracking branch like origin/main or origin/feature. It’s stored in config as branch.<name>.remote and branch.<name>.merge. You can see it with: git status It often says: “Your branch is ahead of ‘origin/feature’ by N commits.” Or: git branch -vv Upstream matters for: - git pull (where to fetch/merge/rebase from) - git push (default destination) - git rebase without arguments (if configured) can use upstream in certain contexts (like rebase --onto upstream) But generally, when you run git rebase main explicitly, upstream is less important.
If I want to rebase my current branch onto its upstream, what command is that?

If your branch tracks an upstream, you can do: git fetch git rebase @{u} @{u} means “upstream of the current branch.” Or: git pull --rebase (which fetches and rebases automatically) Using fetch + rebase @{u} is explicit and avoids surprises about remote changes.
I sometimes see @{-1} too. What’s that?

@{-1} refers to the previous branch you were on (like “last branch”). It’s handy for toggling: git checkout @{-1} It’s not directly rebase-related, but it can help navigate without remembering names. There’s also: - HEAD@{1} etc. in reflog context (previous positions of HEAD) Different “@{}” syntaxes refer to different Git concepts: upstream, reflog entries, previous branches.
If I rebase and then realize I need one of the old commits, can I cherry-pick it from reflog?

Yes. Steps: 1) Find the old commit hash in reflog: git reflog 2) Cherry-pick it: git cherry-pick <oldhash> Or create a temporary branch at the old state: git branch oldstate <oldhash> then browse/log it. Because the old commits often still exist, you can recover content easily.
Are there cases where rebase changes code semantics compared to merge?

Usually the final combined code can differ depending on conflict resolution choices, but even without conflicts, there can be differences due to how patches apply. In theory: - Merge results in a combined tree that includes both sides’ changes with a three-way merge, possibly resulting in a different combination than replaying commits one by one. - Rebase applies commits sequentially onto a new base, which can lead to different conflict points and different resolutions. Even if both end states are logically intended to be “main + feature,” the mechanical steps differ. For example, if a commit depends on an earlier file state, rebasing might cause it to apply differently or require conflict resolution in a different place than a merge would. So yes, rebase can change how conflicts surface and thus can affect semantics if resolutions differ. That’s why tests matter after either operation.
If I resolve conflicts during rebase, should I prefer “ours” or “theirs”? I always get mixed up.

The meaning of “ours” and “theirs” depends on the operation: - During merge (git merge otherbranch): - ours = current branch (the one you’re on) - theirs = the branch being merged in - During rebase: - It’s more confusing: when rebasing, Git is replaying commits from your branch onto a new base. In conflicts during rebase, “ours” often refers to the new base (the branch you’re rebasing onto), and “theirs” refers to the commit being applied. This can feel inverted. Because of that, relying on “ours/theirs” can be error-prone in rebase conflicts. Better: - read the conflict markers and understand what each side represents, - use diff3 style to see base, - use git checkout --theirs/--ours carefully and verify. If you do use the checkout helpers: git checkout --ours <file> git checkout --theirs <file> then inspect and adjust.
That explains why I keep choosing the wrong one during rebase. Is there a safer approach?

Safer approaches: 1) Use merge.conflictStyle diff3/zdiff3 so you see base/ours/theirs. 2) Use a merge tool (git mergetool) that labels sides clearly. 3) Avoid blanket “ours/theirs” for whole files unless you’re certain; resolve at hunk level. 4) After resolving, run: git diff to review the final file content before continuing. Also, remember you can always abort the rebase if you think you made a mess.
What’s the best mergetool to use?

It depends on your preferences and OS. Common ones: - vimdiff (built-in-ish, good if you like terminal) - meld (GUI) - kdiff3 - beyond compare Git configuration example: git config --global merge.tool meld git config --global mergetool.meld.cmd 'meld "$LOCAL" "$MERGED" "$REMOTE" --output "$MERGED"' But setting up mergetools can be fiddly. If you’re comfortable in an editor, many editors have merge conflict resolution UIs too.
I want to understand “rebase is like cherry-picking a range.” Is that accurate?

Yes, conceptually. A simple rebase of feature onto main is roughly equivalent to: - find the list of commits on feature not on main, - then cherry-pick them one by one onto main (or onto a temporary branch at main), - then move feature pointer to the new tip. This is why rebase conflicts can happen per commit, like cherry-picks. The difference is: rebase automates the range selection and manages the branch pointer updates for you.
Are there any gotchas with rebase and submodules?

Yes, submodules can add complexity because a submodule update is represented as a change to a gitlink (a specific commit SHA) in the superproject. If your branch updates a submodule pointer and main also updates it, merges/rebases can conflict on that SHA. Resolving means choosing which submodule commit the superproject should reference, and possibly updating the submodule itself. During rebase, you might get conflicts in the submodule “file” entry. You’ll need to: - decide correct submodule commit, - update submodule state (git submodule update), - then stage the submodule pointer in the superproject. It’s manageable but can be confusing if you haven’t dealt with submodule pointers before.
What about rebase and signed commits? Does rebasing break signatures?

Yes, rebasing recreates commits, so any GPG/SSH signatures on the original commits won’t carry over. The new commits will be unsigned unless you sign them during rebase. You can sign during rebase: - Configure commit signing: git config --global commit.gpgsign true - Or run rebase with: git rebase --gpg-sign But note: if you’re rewriting many commits, signing each can be time-consuming depending on how your signing is configured. Merge doesn’t alter existing commits, so their signatures remain valid. A merge commit would need its own signature if you sign merges.
Good to know. Another thing: I sometimes have local changes not committed when I want to rebase. What should I do?

Rebase requires a clean working tree (no unstaged or uncommitted changes), unless you use autostash. Options: 1) Commit your changes (even as WIP) then rebase; later amend/squash. 2) Stash: git stash push -m "wip" git rebase main git stash pop Resolve any conflicts from applying stash. 3) Use autostash: git rebase --autostash main This automatically stashes before rebase and pops after. Autostash is convenient, but still verify the result after pop, especially if conflicts occur.
Is autostash safe? I worry about losing changes.

Generally safe; it uses the stash mechanism, which is fairly robust. But like any operation that temporarily stores changes, there are edge cases: - If stash pop conflicts, you may need to resolve manually. - If something goes wrong, you can often recover the stash from: git stash list Even with autostash, Git typically leaves the stash entry if pop fails, so you don’t lose it. If changes are important, you can also create a patch as extra safety: git diff > /tmp/wip.patch But in normal usage, stash/autostash is fine.
Can you explain “git pull --rebase” vs “git fetch; git rebase” in terms of what refs change?

Sure. git fetch: - Updates remote-tracking branches: origin/main, origin/feature, etc. - Does not change your local branch pointers (main, feature), unless you fetch into them explicitly (rare). git rebase origin/main (while on feature): - Rewrites feature’s commits and moves feature pointer to new tip. - Updates HEAD accordingly. - Does not change origin/main. git pull --rebase: - First does fetch (updates origin/*) - Then rebases current branch onto its upstream (often origin/<current> or origin/main depending on tracking) So it changes: - origin/* (from fetch) - your current local branch (from rebase) It doesn’t change other local branches.
Sometimes I rebase onto origin/main but my local main is behind. Does that matter?

Not really, as long as you’re intentionally rebasing onto origin/main (the up-to-date remote-tracking branch). Your local main being behind just means you haven’t updated it. It can still be useful to keep local main updated for convenience: git checkout main git pull --ff-only But it’s not required for rebasing feature onto origin/main.
What is the “fork point” concept again? When would I need it?

Fork point is Git’s attempt to find where your branch actually diverged from upstream, using reflog info, even if upstream moved in a way that makes merge-base misleading (like if upstream was rebased). Example: you branched from main at commit B. Later main was rebased and no longer contains B, so merge-base(feature, main) might be older than B and include extra commits. Then a normal rebase could replay too much. Using: git rebase --fork-point main or configuring rebase.forkPoint true can help Git choose the correct starting point for replay. It’s mostly relevant in histories where the upstream branch itself is rewritten.
If upstream is rewritten, doesn’t that already cause problems?

Yes, rewriting a widely used base branch is inherently disruptive because everyone’s history references those old commits. But in contexts where it happens (e.g., rewriting a topic branch), fork-point can make local rebases behave more intuitively. Even if you don’t rewrite main, you might encounter fork-point issues when rebasing a branch that tracks a remote branch that was force-pushed.
I think I’m getting it. But can you summarize a “decision tree” for rebase vs merge?

Decision tree: 1) Are the commits you’d rewrite already depended on elsewhere (other clones, references, or you want stable hashes)? - Yes → prefer merge (or avoid rewriting; maybe merge main into your branch) - No → rebase is fine 2) Do you want a linear history on the mainline? - Yes → rebase topic branch onto main, then fast-forward merge (or squash if you want one commit) - No / you want explicit integration points → merge with a merge commit 3) Do you need to preserve internal merge structure of your branch? - Yes → merge, or rebase with --rebase-merges - No → normal rebase is fine 4) Do you want to keep each commit meaningful? - If your branch has messy WIP commits → interactive rebase (squash/fixup/reword) before integrating
One more nuance: If I merge main into feature repeatedly, does that make later integration harder?

It can, because frequent merges from main into feature create many merge commits, and later when you merge feature back into main, the history graph becomes more complex. Sometimes it’s fine; sometimes it adds noise. Rebasing feature onto main periodically avoids those intermediate merge commits and keeps feature history linear relative to main. That can make later integration smoother and logs cleaner. But merging main into feature has advantages: - It doesn’t rewrite feature history, so no force pushes. - It records the exact points where you integrated main changes. So “harder” depends on what you consider hard: graph complexity vs rewriting.
If I want to avoid force pushing, should I avoid rebase entirely?

You can still rebase locally without force pushing, as long as you don’t push the rebased branch over an existing remote branch that has the old history. To avoid force pushing: - Only rebase branches that haven’t been pushed yet. - Or if pushed, instead of rebasing, merge updates in. If you want to both push early and rebase later without force pushes, that’s not possible because the remote has the old commits and updating it to the rebased commits is a non-fast-forward update. So yes: avoiding force pushes tends to push you toward merge-based integration, or toward delaying pushes until history is finalized.
But delaying pushes feels risky. What if I want backups?

If you want backups without committing to stable history, you can: - push to a separate backup branch name and force push that as needed, - or push frequently but accept that you’ll force push with lease when rebasing. Another approach: push tags or notes? But tags also point to commits and don’t like being rewritten if you rely on them. If you’re backing up for safety, pushing and later force-with-lease is fine if you’re disciplined. Alternatively, you can just rely on multiple local clones or backups, but pushing is simplest.
What about “git reset --soft” vs rebase? Are they related?

They’re different tools but can be used for similar ends (history editing). - git reset moves branch pointers (and optionally index/working tree). It can “uncommit” commits. - --soft: moves HEAD but keeps index and working tree as-is (changes remain staged) - --mixed (default): keeps working tree, resets index - --hard: resets both index and working tree (destructive) - git rebase replays commits onto a new base. For cleaning up last few commits, reset + recommit can be simpler than interactive rebase, but it’s more manual and doesn’t handle complex sequences as cleanly. Example to squash last 3 commits into one: git reset --soft HEAD~3 git commit -m "Combined commit" That rewrites history (new commit), similar to interactive rebase squash.
If I do reset --soft and then commit, does author date change?

The new commit will have a new author and committer date (unless you specify otherwise). The old commits remain in reflog for a while. So yes, it’s rewriting. Interactive rebase preserves author dates by default when replaying commits, but when you squash into a new commit, the resulting commit’s author is typically yours and date is current unless you set author explicitly. Some tools preserve authorship; it depends on how you perform it.
So interactive rebase is “safer” for preserving author info?

For moving commits (without squashing), rebase preserves author info per commit by default. For squashing/fixup, you’re creating a new combined commit, so authorship becomes a single author unless you manually adjust. You can edit the author with: git commit --amend --author="Name <email>" or during rebase with exec commands, but that’s advanced. If preserving multiple authors matters, avoid squashing across different authors, or use merge commits to preserve individual commits.
I think I’ve got enough to choose. But I’d like a concrete personal workflow I can follow day-to-day that avoids footguns.

Here’s a simple, low-footgun workflow that’s consistent: 1) Keep main updated without merge commits: git checkout main git pull --ff-only 2) For a topic branch: git checkout -b feature main 3) While working: - Make meaningful commits. If you need WIP commits, that’s okay. - Periodically update your branch with upstream changes: - If you haven’t pushed feature (or you’re okay force-with-lease): git fetch origin git rebase origin/main - If you want to avoid rewriting: git fetch origin git merge origin/main 4) Before integrating: - Clean up commits if desired: git rebase -i origin/main (squash/fixup/reword) - Run tests. 5) Integrate: - If you rebased and want linear main: git checkout main git merge --ff-only feature - If you merged main into feature and have merge commits: git checkout main git merge feature (will likely create a merge commit) 6) If you rebased after pushing: git push --force-with-lease Key safety checks: - git status before operations - backup branch before big interactive rebase - prefer --force-with-lease
That seems doable. Last question: If I accidentally force-push the wrong branch, is there a recovery?

Often yes, if you act quickly. 1) The remote may still have the old commits reachable via reflog-like mechanisms depending on server, but you can’t rely on that. 2) Locally, you can recover old branch tips via reflog: git reflog If you force-pushed main to the wrong commit, but you had the correct main tip locally earlier, reflog can show it. You can reset back and force push again (with lease), restoring the correct tip. If you overwrote commits that existed only on the remote and nowhere locally, recovery is harder. But usually at least one clone still has them. Practical prevention: - Use --force-with-lease (still can push wrong branch, but less likely to overwrite unexpected remote state). - Double-check: git branch --show-current and: git log -n 3 --oneline before pushing.
Thanks. I’m going to try rebase more, but carefully. If I run into a weird case, can I describe the commit graph and you help choose the right command?

Yes—describe the branches, which commit you want to end up on which base, whether you need to preserve merges, and whether the commits have been pushed. If you can paste output from: git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all -n 30 and git branch -vv I can suggest exact commands and explain what they’ll do.
I’m designing a REST API for a personal project and want feedback on endpoint structure. Is it better to use /users/{id}/posts or /posts?user_id={id} for fetching a user's posts?

Both are valid, and the “better” choice depends on what you want the URL to communicate and how flexible your filtering needs are. - /users/{id}/posts emphasizes a hierarchical relationship: “posts that belong to this user.” It’s great when the primary way to access posts is through the owning user and when that relationship is stable and unambiguous. - /posts?user_id={id} treats “posts” as a top-level collection and “user_id” as one of many possible filters. It’s often better when posts are queried in many dimensions (by tag, date range, status, category, etc.) and you want a consistent filtering model. A practical rule: if the nested path is just a filter, prefer query params. If it represents a sub-resource that only makes sense in the context of the parent, nesting is fine. If you expect a lot of filters beyond user, /posts with query parameters tends to scale better. You can still offer /users/{id}/posts as a convenience alias that internally maps to the same handler.
That makes sense. If I support both, is that considered bad API design? I’m worried about duplication and confusion.

Supporting both isn’t inherently bad, but it adds surface area and potential ambiguity. The main risks: 1) Documentation complexity: two ways to do the same thing invites questions about differences. 2) Divergence over time: you might accidentally add fields/filters/behavior to one and not the other. 3) Cache keys and analytics: duplicates can fragment metrics and caching layers. If you do offer both, make them truly equivalent: - Same response shape, pagination, sorting, filtering semantics. - Same authorization rules and error codes. - Same default sort order. Then position one as canonical in docs, and the other as an alias. For example: - Canonical: GET /posts?user_id=123 - Alias: GET /users/123/posts (documented as a shortcut) Alternatively, you can return a 301/308 redirect from the alias to the canonical, though redirects can be annoying for some clients. Another approach is to keep both but clearly mark one as “preferred”.
I like the idea of canonical + alias. How do you decide which should be canonical?

Pick canonical based on consistency and extensibility. If your API generally treats resources as top-level collections with filtering via query parameters, then /posts?user_id=... should be canonical. It unifies how people think about search/list endpoints. If your API leans heavily into hierarchical organization (e.g., /users/{id}/settings, /users/{id}/tokens, /users/{id}/posts), then /users/{id}/posts might feel more consistent. A good heuristic: - Use nested paths for resources that are “owned” and not meaningfully accessible without the parent (e.g., /users/{id}/sessions). - Use top-level for resources that might be accessed independently (e.g., /posts/{post_id} exists, so posts are a first-class resource; thus /posts?user_id=... is a natural canonical list). Since posts typically have their own identity and can be fetched individually, I’d usually make /posts canonical.
Got it. For list endpoints, what’s a good pagination approach? I’ve seen offset/limit and cursor-based.

Both are common; each has tradeoffs. Offset/limit: - Example: GET /posts?user_id=123&limit=20&offset=40 Pros: - Simple to implement and understand. - Easy to jump to arbitrary pages (offset). Cons: - Can become slow for large offsets in some data stores. - Not stable under inserts/deletes; you can skip or duplicate items if data changes between requests. Cursor-based (a.k.a. keyset pagination): - Example: GET /posts?user_id=123&limit=20&cursor=eyJpZCI6... Pros: - Stable and performant for large datasets. - Works well when new items are added; you get a consistent traversal. Cons: - Harder to implement. - Harder to “jump to page N” (usually not supported). - Requires a deterministic sort order and cursor encoding. If you expect large collections and want stability, cursor-based is better. A hybrid approach is possible: start with offset/limit for simplicity, but design the response in a way that you can add cursors later without breaking clients.
If I do cursor-based, what should the cursor contain? Just the last item ID?

It depends on your sort order. If you sort by a unique, monotonically increasing field (like created_at plus id as a tiebreaker), the cursor should encode the values needed to resume: - Sort: created_at desc, id desc Cursor contains: last_seen_created_at, last_seen_id Then the next page query is “created_at < last_seen_created_at OR (created_at = last_seen_created_at AND id < last_seen_id)”. If you only sort by id desc and id is unique and roughly time-ordered, cursor can contain just last_seen_id. But many systems can’t guarantee that ID ordering matches creation time, and clients often want “newest first”, so created_at is common. Implementation detail: encode the cursor as an opaque string (e.g., base64-encoded JSON) so you can change internals later. Don’t expose raw SQL fragments or anything that could be manipulated into injection. Also sign it (HMAC) if you want to prevent tampering.
Opaque cursor sounds good. How do you structure the pagination metadata in the response? Like next_cursor, has_more, total_count?

For cursor pagination, typical fields: - data: array of items - paging: { next_cursor, prev_cursor?, has_more } Example: { "data": [ ... ], "paging": { "next_cursor": "abc123", "has_more": true } } Total count is tricky: - It can be expensive to compute accurately with filters. - It can be misleading because counts can change between requests. - It’s often unnecessary for infinite scroll patterns. If you do provide total_count, consider making it optional via an include flag: GET /posts?user_id=123&limit=20&include=total_count Or return an estimated count with a note in docs. For offset pagination, total_count is more common, but you still may want to make it optional.
What about sorting? Should it be sort=created_at or order_by=created_at&order=desc?

A common, flexible pattern is: - sort=created_at - order=asc|desc But that doesn’t support multi-field sorts. Another common pattern: - sort=created_at,-id Where “-” indicates descending. Or: - order_by=created_at - direction=desc If you want multi-sort and compactness, sort=created_at,-id is nice. But you must document allowed fields and default order. Also consider how sorting interacts with cursor pagination: the cursor must encode the fields used for sorting. So if you allow arbitrary sorting, you either: - restrict sorting to a small set of known patterns, or - include sort in cursor and validate it, or - require clients to send the same sort params with cursor and reject mismatch. A pragmatic approach: support a limited set of sort keys for each endpoint and define defaults.
How do you feel about using field selection like ?fields=id,title,created_at to reduce payload?

It’s useful, especially if resources have large optional fields. But it adds complexity in: - Caching (different field sets produce different responses). - Documentation and testing (many combinations). - Authorization (fields might have different visibility rules). If you add fields selection: - Make it opt-in: default response includes a sensible set. - Validate allowed fields per resource. - Consider “sparse fieldsets” style: fields[posts]=id,title,created_at (popular in JSON:API). That’s more verbose but avoids collisions when including related resources. If you don’t need it yet, you can delay it. Often, compression (gzip/br) and careful default payloads are enough.
Speaking of related resources, what’s your opinion on embedding vs separate calls? For example, including user info with each post.

There are a few patterns: 1) Embed minimal fields: Return post with embedded author summary: { "id": "...", "title": "...", "author": { "id": "...", "name": "..." } } Pros: fewer calls, simpler client. Cons: duplication, potential inconsistency. 2) Include via query param: GET /posts?include=author Return posts plus included authors in a separate section (JSON:API style): { "data": [ { post }, ... ], "included": [ { author }, ... ] } Pros: avoids duplication; normalized-ish. Cons: more complex response format. 3) Separate calls: GET /posts then GET /users/{id} Pros: simple resource model. Cons: N+1 round trips unless client batches. A pragmatic compromise: embed a small author object by default (id + display name) and provide include=author_details for more. Or keep author_id in post and let clients fetch separately if needed. Also consider caching: user info might change; embedding can make caches stale.
I’m leaning toward embedding minimal author fields. Should I name it author or user? The resource is /users.

Prefer a name that reflects the role in the context: - For posts, “author” is clearer than “user” because it tells you what the relationship means. - You can still include author_id referencing a user. Example: { "id": "p1", "title": "Hello", "author_id": "u1", "author": { "id": "u1", "name": "..." } } If you do this, ensure consistency across resources: comments might have “author” as well, or “creator”. Pick a convention (author vs creator) and stick to it.
How do you handle filtering? I might want /posts?user_id=...&status=published&tag=api.

Filtering via query parameters is standard. A few tips: - Use consistent naming: user_id, status, tag, created_before, created_after, etc. - Decide whether multiple tags are supported: - tag=api (single) - tag=api&tag=rest (repeated) - tags=api,rest (comma-separated) Repeated params are common and unambiguous. - For ranges: - created_at_gte=... - created_at_lte=... Or: - created_after=... - created_before=... Pick one style. - For “search”: - q=term Define what fields it searches. Also, document how filters combine (usually AND). If you need OR logic, it gets complicated; you might introduce a filter expression syntax, but that’s easy to over-engineer. Many APIs avoid complex boolean logic.
About naming, do you prefer snake_case or camelCase in JSON?

Pick one and be consistent. Common choices: - snake_case: created_at, user_id - camelCase: createdAt, userId snake_case aligns with many backend languages and databases; camelCase aligns with typical JavaScript conventions. Neither is “more RESTful.” If your API is JSON-first and you expect lots of JS clients, camelCase can feel natural. If you want readability and alignment with query params like created_at, snake_case is convenient. One thing: keep query params consistent with JSON field names if possible; it reduces mental mapping. If you do camelCase in JSON, consider also using camelCase in query params.
If I do camelCase in JSON, is it weird to have /users/{id} but fields like userId?

Not weird. Path segments are typically plural nouns and not directly tied to JSON field naming. /users/{id} is conventional. You can still have: - resource: /users - JSON: { "id": "...", "displayName": "...", ... } What matters is internal consistency: don’t mix display_name and displayName in the same API.
Let’s talk about error responses. What’s a good standard shape?

A consistent error envelope helps a lot. A common approach: { "error": { "code": "invalid_request", "message": "limit must be between 1 and 100", "details": [ { "field": "limit", "issue": "out_of_range" } ], "request_id": "..." } } Guidelines: - Use HTTP status codes correctly (400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 429, 500). - Provide a stable machine-readable code (string enum). - Provide a human message. - Provide per-field details for validation errors. - Include request_id for debugging (even if only for logging correlation). For validation, 422 Unprocessable Entity is common; some prefer 400. Pick one and document.
For 404, should I reveal whether a resource exists if the requester isn’t authorized?

Typically no. If authorization depends on ownership/visibility, returning 404 for both “not found” and “not allowed” is common to avoid leaking existence. But it’s a tradeoff: clients get less precise errors. A common pattern: - If the client is authenticated but lacks permission: 404 (to avoid resource enumeration). - If the client is authenticated and resource exists but is forbidden in a non-sensitive way: 403 can be fine. - If unauthenticated: 401. Document your choice. Also ensure consistent behavior across endpoints.
What about id format? Should I use numeric IDs or UUIDs? Or something like ULIDs?

Tradeoffs: Numeric IDs: - Simple, compact. - Easy to debug. - But sequential IDs can make enumeration easier and leak scale. UUIDv4: - Hard to guess, good for public identifiers. - Larger and less readable. - Index performance can be worse than sequential IDs depending on DB. ULID / UUIDv7: - Sortable by time, good for pagination and database locality. - Still globally unique and non-guessable enough for many cases. If IDs are exposed publicly, many choose UUID/ULID to reduce enumeration risks. If you want cursor pagination and time ordering, ULID/UUIDv7 are attractive. You can also use internal numeric IDs and expose public IDs separately (like “public_id”), but that adds complexity.
If I use ULIDs, should the API treat them case-insensitively?

ULIDs are typically encoded in Crockford Base32 and are case-insensitive in principle, but many libraries output uppercase. In APIs, it’s safest to: - Accept case-insensitive input. - Normalize to uppercase in responses (or lowercase, but pick one). Also validate length and characters to give clear 400 errors for malformed IDs.
How would you structure versioning? /v1/posts or header-based?

The common options: 1) URI versioning: /v1/posts Pros: simple, visible, easy to route. Cons: “bakes” version into URLs; some dislike it philosophically. 2) Header versioning: Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v1+json Pros: cleaner URLs; can negotiate versions. Cons: harder to test manually; more client complexity. 3) Query param: /posts?api_version=1 Pros: easy to use. Cons: can be messy and cache-unfriendly. For most personal projects and many production APIs, /v1 is pragmatic. Combine it with a policy: don’t bump versions for additive changes; only for breaking changes.
If I do /v1, should it be /api/v1 or just /v1?

Either. /api/v1 is common when the domain also serves non-API pages or you want a namespace. If it’s API-only, /v1 is fine. The key is consistency and avoiding future collisions. Many go with: - Base: /api/v1 Because it leaves room for /health, /metrics, etc., without mixing.
For creating resources, do you prefer POST /posts with server-generated ID, or PUT /posts/{id} with client-generated ID?

Usually: - POST /posts to create with server-generated ID. - PUT /posts/{id} to replace/update an existing resource at a known ID (idempotent). Client-generated IDs via PUT can be useful for offline-first clients or when you want idempotent creation. But it complicates validation and can create conflicts. A common compromise: - POST /posts with optional idempotency key header to avoid duplicate creates on retries. - Server returns the created resource with id. If you need idempotent create without extra headers, you can support PUT with a client-provided ID, but document it clearly and handle conflicts (409).
Tell me more about idempotency keys. How should I implement that?

Conceptually: - Client sends POST /posts with header Idempotency-Key: <unique key> - Server stores the key along with the resulting response (status + body) for a certain TTL. - If the same key is seen again with the same endpoint and same authenticated principal, return the stored response instead of creating a duplicate. Implementation details: - Scope the key to (client identity, endpoint). If you don’t have client identity, scope it to whatever auth mechanism you have. - Enforce a reasonable retention window (e.g., 24 hours). - Decide behavior if same key is reused with different request body: - reject with 409 (idempotency key replay with different payload), or - treat as same and return original response (but that can hide bugs). Most robust: store a hash of the request body and reject mismatches.
For updates, PATCH vs PUT. What would you choose?

If you want partial updates, PATCH is appropriate. PUT is “replace the entire resource representation” (though many APIs treat it as partial in practice). A clean approach: - PUT /posts/{id} expects the full resource (or at least all mutable fields) and replaces them. - PATCH /posts/{id} accepts partial fields and updates only those. For PATCH, define semantics: - JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396): simple; null means remove field. - JSON Patch (RFC 6902): operations array; more powerful but more complex. Most APIs use merge patch-like behavior with a plain JSON object. If you do, document how to clear fields (e.g., send null, or use a separate “unset” mechanism).
If I allow null to clear a field, how do I distinguish “not provided” vs “set to null”?

In JSON, absence vs null are distinct: - Field absent: don’t change it (PATCH). - Field present with null: clear it. This works well as long as null is not a valid value for that field. If null can be valid, you need a different mechanism (e.g., explicit “clearFields”: ["subtitle"]). Also ensure your server-side validation distinguishes missing keys from keys present with null.
Let’s talk about HTTP status codes for create/update/delete. What’s best practice?

Common choices: Create (POST /posts): - 201 Created - Location: /posts/{id} - Response body: created resource (often helpful) Update: - PUT/PATCH /posts/{id} - 200 OK with updated resource, or - 204 No Content if you don’t return body Returning the updated representation is convenient and avoids an extra GET. Delete: - DELETE /posts/{id} - 204 No Content If deleting an already-deleted resource: - 404 Not Found, or - 204 (idempotent delete) Both exist; pick one and document. Many APIs choose 404 to indicate it doesn’t exist, but idempotent delete returning 204 can simplify clients.
For 204 deletes, what about soft deletes? Like marking deletedAt.

If you soft-delete, you can still return 204 for the delete operation. Internally you set deletedAt, and normal list/get endpoints exclude deleted items by default. You may want: - GET /posts?include_deleted=true (admin-like use) or - GET /posts/{id}?include_deleted=true But be careful: “deleted” resources can complicate uniqueness constraints and restore operations. If you want restore: - POST /posts/{id}/restore Or: - PATCH /posts/{id} { "deletedAt": null } (if you allow it) Also define whether deleted resources can be re-created with same slug/title, etc.
Should I model actions like /posts/{id}/publish as a subresource or do PATCH with status=published?

Prefer state transitions via PATCH when they’re simple field changes: - PATCH /posts/{id} { "status": "published" } Use action endpoints when: - The action is not a simple state field, or - It triggers complex behavior with parameters (e.g., “rebuild index”, “send notifications”), or - You want to represent a command that isn’t naturally a resource property. If you do action endpoints, keep them verb-y but consistent: - POST /posts/{id}/publish - POST /posts/{id}/unpublish Even then, many APIs still prefer PATCH to keep the resource model clean.
For status transitions, how do I prevent invalid transitions? Like draft -> archived directly.

Enforce a state machine server-side: - Define allowed transitions: draft -> published, published -> archived, etc. - On invalid transition, return 409 Conflict or 422 with a structured error explaining allowed transitions. Also consider concurrency: if two clients update status simultaneously, you may need optimistic locking: - ETag / If-Match with versioning, or - A “version” field clients include.
Can you explain ETags and If-Match for optimistic concurrency?

Sure. The idea is: clients update only if they’re acting on the latest representation. Flow: 1) Client GET /posts/{id} Response includes: - ETag: "v3" (or a hash) 2) Client PATCH /posts/{id} with header: - If-Match: "v3" Server checks current ETag: - If it matches, apply update and return new representation with new ETag. - If it doesn’t match, return 412 Precondition Failed. This prevents “lost updates.” You can also use If-None-Match for conditional GETs and caching. Implementation: - ETag can be a version number, updated_at timestamp, or hash of content. - Version number is simplest if you store it.
If I do ETags, does that complicate caching?

It can help caching. ETags enable conditional requests: - Client sends If-None-Match: "etag" - Server returns 304 Not Modified if unchanged, saving bandwidth. But you should think about: - Whether responses are cacheable (Cache-Control). - Whether ETags are stable across serialization differences (ordering, whitespace). If ETag is based on a version field, it’s stable and avoids hashing. If you have personalized content, be careful with shared caches; set appropriate cache headers (often private).
For authentication, I’m considering simple bearer tokens. Any advice on how to design around auth without overcomplicating?

If you’re doing bearer tokens: - Use Authorization: Bearer <token> - Return 401 for missing/invalid token, with WWW-Authenticate: Bearer - Return 403 when authenticated but not permitted (or 404 in some cases as discussed). Design considerations: - Token scopes/permissions: even a simple “role” or “scopes” claim can help. - Rate limiting: 429 with Retry-After. - Consistent error codes for auth failures: token_expired, invalid_token, insufficient_scope. Even if you keep auth simple, keep your API endpoints consistent with future expansion: e.g., don’t bake “admin” into URL paths if it can be permissions-based.
Rate limiting is a good point. How should I expose rate limit info? Headers?

Headers are common: - X-RateLimit-Limit: 1000 - X-RateLimit-Remaining: 42 - X-RateLimit-Reset: 1712345678 (epoch seconds) Or the newer standardized-ish headers: - RateLimit-Limit - RateLimit-Remaining - RateLimit-Reset When limited: - 429 Too Many Requests - Retry-After: seconds or HTTP date - Optional body with error code and message. Document whether limits are per token, per IP, per route group, etc.
Should I separate admin endpoints under /admin or just rely on permissions?

Prefer relying on permissions, not URL shape. URL namespaces like /admin can be useful organizationally, but it can also mislead clients into thinking it’s a separate auth scheme. If you do add admin-only operations, you can still keep them in normal resources: - DELETE /users/{id} - GET /posts?include_deleted=true And enforce permissions. If you want to reduce accidental discovery, /admin can be okay, but don’t treat it as security.
Back to endpoints: for comments on posts, do you prefer /posts/{id}/comments or /comments?post_id={id}?

Same reasoning as posts/users. Comments are a first-class resource if: - You can GET /comments/{id} - You might search comments across posts (by author, date, moderation status) Then /comments with filters scales well. But /posts/{id}/comments is a very intuitive convenience endpoint. Many APIs provide: - Canonical: /comments?post_id=... - Alias: /posts/{id}/comments If your API has a lot of relationships, nesting can become deep. Avoid /users/{id}/posts/{postId}/comments/{commentId} unless comment IDs are not globally unique. Prefer global IDs and shallow paths.
How do you model “likes” on a post? Is it /posts/{id}/likes with POST/DELETE?

A common pattern is to treat “like” as a relationship between a user and a post. Options: 1) Subresource: - POST /posts/{id}/likes (current user likes) - DELETE /posts/{id}/likes (current user unlikes) - GET /posts/{id}/likes?limit=... (list who liked, if allowed) Pros: simple. Cons: ambiguous if you need to like on behalf of another user (usually you don’t). 2) Explicit resource: - POST /likes { postId: ..., userId: ... } - DELETE /likes/{likeId} Pros: more general. Cons: more steps. For most cases, (1) is easiest. If “current user” is implied by auth, you don’t need userId in the path. Return 201 on like create, 204 on unlike.
If I do POST /posts/{id}/likes, what do I return? The like object or the post?

Usually return the created “like” representation or minimal confirmation: - 201 Created with body: { "postId": "...", "userId": "...", "createdAt": "..." } And Location header pointing to a like resource if you have one, like /posts/{id}/likes/{userId} or /likes/{likeId}. If you don’t want to model likes as independent resources, you can return 204, but 201 with a body can be useful. Also decide idempotency: - If user already liked, you can return 200 OK with existing like, or 204, or 409. Many choose idempotent behavior: POST returns 200/201 but doesn’t duplicate.
For idempotent likes, should POST be idempotent? Technically it isn’t.

Correct: POST is not required to be idempotent, but you can implement it idempotently for convenience. Another option is PUT: - PUT /posts/{id}/likes/me - DELETE /posts/{id}/likes/me PUT is idempotent by semantics and communicates “ensure this like exists”. If you have a concept of “me”, you can: - PUT /posts/{id}/likes/{userId} (but then clients can target other users, which you likely don’t want) So /me works if your API supports it: - /users/me - /posts/{id}/likes/me If you don’t like /me, you can keep POST idempotent in practice and document it.
Is /users/me a good idea generally?

Yes, it’s a common convenience endpoint: - GET /users/me - PATCH /users/me It avoids clients needing to store the user ID separately. Just make sure: - It requires authentication. - It returns 401 if not authenticated. - It behaves like /users/{id} but with id resolved from token.
For search endpoints, would you do GET /search?type=posts&q=... or separate /posts/search?

Prefer keeping search within the resource collection: - GET /posts?q=... (if “q” is search term) Or: - GET /posts/search?q=... (less common, but explicit) A global /search endpoint can be useful for multi-type search, but it complicates response shapes and pagination because results may be heterogeneous. If you do multi-type search, consider: - GET /search?q=...&types=posts,users Response includes results with a “type” field. But if you only need posts search, keep it on /posts.
If I use q on /posts for full-text search, how do I differentiate from filtering by exact title?

Use distinct parameters: - q=... for full-text search across multiple fields. - title=... for exact match (or maybe title_eq, title_like). Document the semantics: - q uses tokenization, stemming, etc., depending on your implementation. - title is exact and case-sensitive/insensitive. If you foresee many filter operators, you can adopt a suffix convention: - title_eq=... - title_contains=... - createdAt_gte=... But don’t go overboard unless needed.
Let’s discuss response envelopes. Do you recommend always wrapping in {data: ...} or returning raw arrays/objects?

Wrapping has benefits: - You can add metadata (paging, links) without breaking changes. - Consistent shape: always an object. Example: - GET /posts returns { "data": [...], "paging": {...} } - GET /posts/{id} returns { "data": { ... } } Downside: - More verbose. - Some clients prefer simpler JSON. If you expect to add metadata, wrappers are helpful. If you keep it simple, returning raw can be fine, but you’ll eventually want to add paging info for lists. Many APIs wrap lists but not singletons. That’s also fine: - list: { data: [...], paging: ... } - single: { ... } Consistency matters more than the choice. If you wrap lists but not singletons, document it clearly.
I think I’ll wrap lists but return raw objects for GET by id. Is that acceptable?

Yes, it’s common. Just ensure your error responses are also consistent (usually wrapped). And for create/update where you return a resource, decide whether it’s raw or wrapped; follow the single-resource convention. Example: - GET /posts/{id} -> { ...post... } - POST /posts -> 201 + { ...post... } - GET /posts -> { "data": [ ... ], "paging": ... }
How would you handle HATEOAS links? Worth it?

Usually not necessary unless you explicitly want hypermedia-driven clients. Most practical REST-ish APIs don’t rely on HATEOAS heavily. If you do add links, keep them lightweight: - In list responses, include next/prev URLs. - In resources, optionally include relevant links: { "id": "...", "links": { "self": "/posts/...", "comments": "/posts/.../comments" } } But don’t feel obligated. Clear documentation and predictable URL patterns often suffice.
For content types, should I use application/json only, or versioned vendor media types?

If you’re doing URL versioning (/v1), plain application/json is fine. Vendor media types are useful when: - You want to version via Accept header. - You want to define custom representations. But they add complexity and don’t add much if you already have /v1. So application/json is a reasonable default.
For date/time fields, should I use ISO 8601 strings or timestamps?

Prefer ISO 8601 strings in UTC, e.g.: - "2026-04-02T12:34:56Z" Pros: - Human-readable. - Widely supported. - Less ambiguity than local times.
