If you use timestamps: - Use milliseconds since epoch, but document it. - They’re compact but less readable. ISO 8601 is the most interoperable. Also document whether fractional seconds are included.
If I store timezone-aware times, should I include offsets?

You can, but for APIs it’s simplest to standardize on UTC (“Z”). If you include offsets, clients must handle them correctly; most can. Still, UTC is easier. If you need to represent a user-chosen local time (like “event starts at 9am local”), that’s different: you’d store local time + timezone separately. But for createdAt/updatedAt, UTC is ideal.
For boolean query params, do you prefer true/false or 1/0?
Prefer true/false (case-insensitive). It’s clearer. Document accepted values: - true, false Optionally accept 1/0 for convenience, but normalize internally.
Let’s discuss naming conventions for endpoints. Plural vs singular? /user or /users?

Plural collections are conventional: - /users (collection) - /users/{id} (member) Singular endpoints are usually reserved for singleton resources: - /status - /health - /me (or /users/me) So prefer /users, /posts, /comments, etc.
What about nested singleton resources like /users/{id}/profile?

That’s a good use of singular: profile is a singleton subresource per user. - GET /users/{id}/profile - PATCH /users/{id}/profile Alternatively, you can keep profile fields on the user resource. But if profile is large or has different permissions, splitting makes sense.
If profile is a singleton, should it have its own ID?
Not necessarily. It’s identified by the parent user ID. In representation, you can omit an id or set id = userId. Either way is fine. Just keep the URL stable.
How do you handle file uploads? Like an avatar image.

Options: 1) Direct upload to API via multipart/form-data: - POST /users/me/avatar with multipart Pros: simple. Cons: your API server handles file bytes. 2) Pre-signed upload URL (if you have object storage): - POST /uploads to get { uploadUrl, fileUrl } - Client uploads directly to storage, then PATCH user avatarUrl. Pros: scalable, avoids proxying. Cons: more steps. If you’re keeping it simple, multipart is okay. Return metadata: - 201 with { "url": "...", "width": ..., "height": ... } if you process images. Also validate size/type and return 413 for too large.
For multipart, do I put it under /users/me/avatar or /avatars?

/users/me/avatar expresses the relationship clearly and avoids needing an avatar resource. If you want reuse (e.g., multiple images), then /media or /uploads could be more general. If it’s only avatar, /users/me/avatar is clean: - PUT /users/me/avatar (idempotent replace) - DELETE /users/me/avatar PUT makes sense because avatar is a singleton subresource.
That’s good. For PUT avatar, should it return the updated user or just avatar info?

Either: - Return 200 with { "avatarUrl": "...", "updatedAt": ... } Or return the full updated user if that’s your pattern. Returning avatar info is smaller. If clients often need the updated user object anyway, returning the user can save a call. Pick one and be consistent.
Now about consistent casing: If I do camelCase in JSON, should I do camelCase in query params too?

Yes, that reduces friction: - createdAtGte=... - userId=... But some find camelCase query params less readable. Another compromise is: - JSON camelCase - query params snake_case But then you’re mapping between them. If you care about consistency, do camelCase everywhere: - /posts?userId=123&createdAtGte=... Document param names clearly.
I’m worried camelCase query params look odd. Would snake_case query params be acceptable even with camelCase JSON?

It’s acceptable; many APIs do it. Just be consistent within query params: - user_id, created_at_gte, include_deleted If you do this, consider also using snake_case for JSON to avoid mismatches, unless you have a strong preference for camelCase. Either way, it’s not a correctness issue—just ergonomics.
Let’s say I choose snake_case for everything. For enums like status, should I use lowercase strings?

Lowercase snake_case strings are a good choice: - "draft" - "published" - "archived" Avoid numeric enums in public APIs because they’re not self-descriptive and can change meaning. Also document the allowed values and what they mean.
For validation errors on enums, should the error include allowed values?

Yes, that’s helpful: { "error": { "code": "invalid_enum", "message": "status must be one of: draft, published, archived", "details": [ { "field": "status", "allowed": ["draft","published","archived"] } ] } } Be careful not to leak sensitive internal options, but for public enums it’s fine.
How do you handle partial failures in batch endpoints? Like POST /posts/batch.

Batch endpoints can be tricky. If you need them, define clear semantics. Options: 1) All-or-nothing: - If any item fails, whole request fails (400/422) with details. Pros: simpler consistency. Cons: clients must retry after fixing. 2) Partial success: - Return 207 Multi-Status (WebDAV) or 200 with per-item results. Pros: efficient. Cons: more complex. If you do partial success, include per-item status: { "results": [ { "index": 0, "status": 201, "data": {...} }, { "index": 1, "status": 422, "error": {...} } ] } 207 is technically appropriate but some clients aren’t used to it. Returning 200 with per-item statuses is common.
Would you recommend having batch endpoints at all?

Only if you have a clear need (e.g., reducing round trips for mobile clients, bulk import). They increase complexity in validation, rate limits, and transactional behavior. If you add them, keep them limited and well-documented.
What about GraphQL instead of REST for this? I’m sticking with REST but curious.

GraphQL shines when clients need flexible shapes and deep relationships without multiple endpoints. It also shifts complexity to the server (schema, resolvers, N+1, caching). REST is great when: - Resources are fairly well-defined. - Caching and HTTP semantics matter. - You want simpler operational model. Since you’re already thinking in REST terms and want endpoint structure feedback, REST is a solid choice. You can still borrow ideas like field selection and includes.
For documentation, do you recommend OpenAPI? Any pitfalls?

OpenAPI is very helpful. Pitfalls: - Keeping spec in sync with implementation. - Overly generic schemas that don’t capture constraints (like enum values, min/max). - Not documenting error responses consistently. Tips: - Define reusable components: Error, Paging, Post, UserSummary. - Use examples liberally. - Specify pagination params and response fields. - If you use cursor pagination, document cursor as opaque and provide example cursors. - Document auth security schemes.
For OpenAPI, do you define separate schemas for create vs response? Like PostCreate vs Post?

Often yes, because: - Response has read-only fields: id, created_at, updated_at. - Create request might require fields that are optional later, or vice versa. So you might have: - Post (full representation) - PostCreateRequest - PostUpdateRequest (PATCH) You can reduce duplication by composing schemas (allOf) if your tooling supports it well, but sometimes explicit schemas are clearer.
Let’s talk about PUT vs PATCH for updates. If I only implement one, which would you pick?

PATCH is usually more ergonomic for clients because they can send only changed fields. If you implement only PUT, clients need to send full representation or risk accidental field resets. So if choosing one: - Implement PATCH for partial updates. - Define clear rules for nulls and missing fields. You can still support PUT later if you want.
For PATCH, should I allow updating nested objects, like profile fields inside user?

You can, but define how deep merges work. If you accept a JSON object and do a shallow merge, nested objects become tricky: - PATCH { "profile": { "bio": "x" } } Does that replace profile entirely or just bio? If you want predictable behavior: - Use JSON Merge Patch semantics (deep merge-ish but with specific rules). Or: - Require clients to patch nested subresources: - PATCH /users/{id}/profile Splitting nested structures into subresources often simplifies patch behavior.
Good point. I’ll probably do /users/me/profile. For list endpoints, should I include “links” like next URL or just next_cursor?

With cursor pagination, returning next_cursor is enough. Some APIs also include a computed next URL for convenience: "paging": { "next_cursor": "...", "next_url": "/posts?limit=20&cursor=..." } But next_url can be tricky if base URLs differ. If you do include it, make it relative or absolute consistently. Returning next_cursor is the most minimal and flexible.
For filtering and sorting, should I support unknown params or reject them?

Rejecting unknown params is usually better: - Helps catch typos (e.g., limt=20 silently ignored). - Makes client bugs more visible. Return 400 with an error like: unknown_query_parameter: "limt" If you want forward compatibility, you can allow unknown params but ignore them, but that can hide errors. A compromise is to reject unknown params on write endpoints and be permissive on reads, but that’s inconsistent. I’d generally reject unknown params everywhere.
How about default limits? Should I default to 20? 50?

Pick a sensible default and a max. Common: - default limit: 20 or 25 - max limit: 100 Document it. Also decide behavior if limit is omitted or too large: - If omitted, use default. - If > max, either clamp to max or return 400. Returning 400 is clearer; clamping can surprise clients.
On list endpoints, should I return items sorted by created_at desc by default?

Yes, “newest first” is common for posts. For other resources, choose what’s most natural: - Users might default to created_at desc or name asc. - Comments might default to created_at asc within a post (conversation order). Just document defaults and ensure cursor pagination uses that ordering.
For comments, I might want oldest-first. If I do cursor pagination with created_at asc, is that okay?

Yes. Cursor pagination works for either direction. Your cursor query changes accordingly: - For asc: next page is created_at > last_seen_created_at (with id tie-breaker). Just be consistent: if default is asc, cursor continues forward in that order. If you allow order=desc, then cursor must encode direction or require clients to specify same order with cursor.
Another question: should I return 200 with empty array or 404 when a user has no posts?

Return 200 with an empty array. 404 is for “the endpoint/resource doesn’t exist,” not “collection is empty.” So: GET /posts?user_id=123 -> 200 + { data: [] } If the user_id doesn’t exist and you want to validate that filter, you could still return empty results; most APIs do. If you want to signal invalid user_id, you’d need additional semantics, but it’s typically not worth it.
If user_id filter is invalid format, like not a ULID, should that be 400?
Yes. If the filter value is malformed, 400 is appropriate. If it’s well-formed but doesn’t correspond to an existing user, returning empty results is reasonable.
For endpoints that accept body JSON, do you require Content-Type header?

Yes. Require: - Content-Type: application/json If missing or wrong, return 415 Unsupported Media Type. Also consider requiring Accept: application/json, but many APIs default to JSON if Accept is missing. If you want strictness, return 406 Not Acceptable for unsupported Accept headers, but it’s often unnecessary.
What about trailing slashes? /posts vs /posts/?

Pick one canonical form and be consistent. Many APIs treat them equivalently, but that can cause duplicate routes and caching differences. Common approach: - Canonical: no trailing slash (/posts) - Redirect /posts/ -> /posts (308 Permanent Redirect) Or just accept both but generate links without trailing slash.
Do you recommend using nouns only in paths? Like avoid /getPosts?

Yes. Use nouns for resources and HTTP verbs for actions: - GET /posts - POST /posts Avoid RPC-style /getPosts. It’s clearer and leverages HTTP semantics. For non-CRUD operations, action endpoints are the exception, but keep them minimal.
For “feed” endpoints, like a timeline, would you do /feed or /posts/feed?

If “feed” is essentially a specialized view of posts, you can do: - GET /feed Or: - GET /posts/feed /feed is a singleton-ish collection that returns posts from multiple sources. /posts/feed suggests it’s a sub-collection of posts. Either is fine; I’d lean /feed if it’s a top-level concept, and document it as returning post resources with pagination. Alternatively, keep it as /posts with a filter: - GET /posts?feed=true But that’s a bit vague.
If /feed returns posts, should it share the same response schema as /posts list?

Ideally yes. Reuse the same “post summary” representation, same paging structure, etc. That reduces cognitive load. If feed includes additional fields (like “reason” why the post appears), you can extend the feed item: { "post": { ... }, "reason": "followed_user" } But then it’s not exactly the same as /posts. If you need that, consider /feed returning “feed_items” resources.
Interesting. I might keep it simple and return posts only. Another thing: should I include “expand” parameter to embed related resources?

“expand” is similar to “include”. If you already have include=author, stick with one term. “include” is common; “expand” is also common. Don’t have both. Define allowed includes per endpoint: - include=author,comments_count Or: - include=author and counts always included. Be careful: including deeply nested resources can blow up payloads. Keep includes shallow and controlled.
For counts like comments_count, likes_count, should they be fields on post?

Yes, they’re useful for list views: { "id": "...", "title": "...", "comments_count": 12, "likes_count": 5 } Just document whether they’re eventually consistent (they often are). If computing counts is expensive, you can: - Precompute counters. - Or only include counts when requested via include=counts.
If counts are eventually consistent, should I mention that in the API docs?
Yes. It sets expectations and avoids confusion when a client creates a comment and the count doesn’t update immediately. If you can make them strongly consistent, great, but it’s often not necessary.
For delete comment, should it reduce comments_count immediately?
Ideally yes from a user perspective, but if your system updates counters asynchronously, it might lag. If you can update counters transactionally, do it. Otherwise, document eventual consistency.
Let’s talk about testing the API design. Any suggestions on how to validate the endpoint structure?

A few practical checks: - Resource discoverability: can you guess endpoints from names? - Consistency: do list endpoints behave similarly (pagination, filtering, sorting)? - Error consistency: same envelope and codes everywhere. - Naming: no ambiguous fields (user vs author). - Avoid deep nesting: keep URLs shallow. - Simulate client workflows: create post, list posts, update, comment, like, delete—ensure it feels coherent. Also, generate an OpenAPI spec early and try a client generator or at least a mock client to see if the API is pleasant to consume.
For OpenAPI, how do you represent cursor pagination?

In OpenAPI: - Query params: limit (integer), cursor (string), maybe sort. - Response schema includes paging.next_cursor (string, nullable), paging.has_more (boolean). Example: GET /posts parameters: - name: limit, in: query, schema: { type: integer, minimum: 1, maximum: 100 } - name: cursor, in: query, schema: { type: string } Response: { data: Post[], paging: { next_cursor: string|null, has_more: boolean } } Also include examples showing first request without cursor and subsequent with cursor.
Another question: should I use 422 or 400 for validation errors?

Either is acceptable; pick one consistently. Common patterns: - 400 for malformed JSON, missing required params, invalid query params. - 422 for semantically invalid body (e.g., title too long, status invalid). Some APIs just use 400 for all client-side errors. If you want a bit more nuance, split as above. If you do, document it.
If JSON body can’t be parsed, that’s 400, right?
Yes: - 400 Bad Request With error code like invalid_json. And include a message like “Malformed JSON” without echoing the entire body.
For 401 responses, should I include WWW-Authenticate?
Yes, it’s part of the spec and helps some clients: WWW-Authenticate: Bearer realm="api", error="invalid_token" You can keep it simple: WWW-Authenticate: Bearer
For CORS, any API design considerations?

If you expect browser clients: - Support OPTIONS preflight. - Set Access-Control-Allow-Origin appropriately. - Allow Authorization header. - Expose any custom headers you want clients to read (like RateLimit-*). Design-wise, avoid relying on headers that browsers can’t easily set. Bearer token in Authorization is fine.
For correlation IDs, should clients send X-Request-Id or server generate?

You can support both: - If client sends X-Request-Id, propagate it (validate length/charset). - If not, generate one. Return it in response header (X-Request-Id) and optionally in error body as request_id. This helps debugging without requiring any personal context.
Going back to endpoint structure: what about /users/{id}/posts/{post_id}? Is that redundant if post_id is globally unique?

Yes, usually redundant. If post_id is globally unique, /posts/{post_id} is enough. Nested member endpoints can imply the post ID is only unique within the user, or that you’re enforcing the relationship via URL. If you want to ensure the post belongs to the user, you can still check authorization/ownership on /posts/{post_id}. If you include both /users/{id}/posts/{post_id} and /posts/{post_id}, you again have duplication risks. I’d keep: - /posts/{post_id} - /posts?user_id={id} (or alias /users/{id}/posts for list only)
That clarifies it. For “my posts”, should I do /posts?user_id=me or /users/me/posts?

/users/me/posts is clearer. Using user_id=me is a bit hacky because user_id usually expects an ID format. So: - GET /users/me/posts (alias) Or canonical: - GET /posts?user_id=<actual_id> (but clients may not know it) If you want to avoid /users/me/posts, you can add: - GET /posts?mine=true But that’s less explicit. I’d do /users/me/posts as a convenience.
If I do /users/me/posts, should it support the same filters as /posts?user_id=...?
Yes. Treat it as equivalent, just with implicit user_id. So it should accept: - limit, cursor, sort, status, tag, etc. And return the same response schema.
For tags, should I model them as /tags and /posts?tag=... or nested /tags/{id}/posts?

Tags are often first-class resources: - GET /tags - GET /tags/{tag_id} - GET /posts?tag=api Optionally alias: - GET /tags/{tag_id}/posts If tags have additional metadata (description, color), having /tags makes sense. If tags are just strings, you might skip /tags and treat tags as strings on posts. If you do have /tags, decide whether tag identifier is a slug (e.g., “api-design”) or an ID. Slug is nicer for humans, but then you need to handle renames.
I’m thinking tags are strings. Then /posts?tag=api is fine. If I want multiple tags, should it mean AND or OR?

Decide and document. Common choices: - Repeated tag params means OR: tag=api&tag=rest returns posts with any of those tags. - Or define tags_all=... for AND and tags_any=... for OR. If you only support one behavior, OR is often more forgiving. AND can be useful for narrowing. You can support both with explicit params: - tags_any=api,rest - tags_all=api,rest
For search + tags, should q combine with tags via AND?
Typically yes: filters combine via AND. So q narrows within the tag-filtered set. Document it.
Let’s discuss HTTP methods for listing: always GET, right?

Yes for retrieval. Avoid POST for search unless: - Query is too complex/large for URL limits, or - You need to send a complex filter object. If you do need complex search, you can offer: - POST /posts/search with JSON body filter But that sacrifices cacheability and standard semantics. Many APIs still do it for advanced filtering. Start with GET if possible.
If I do POST /posts/search, should it be /posts/search or /search/posts?

/posts/search reads as “search within posts” and keeps resource grouping. /search/posts is also okay but introduces a /search namespace. I’d pick /posts/search if you’re keeping search scoped per resource.
For POST /posts/search, how would you structure the filter body?

Keep it simple and explicit: { "filter": { "user_id": "u1", "status": ["published","archived"], "tags_any": ["api","rest"], "created_at": { "gte": "...", "lte": "..." } }, "sort": ["-created_at", "-id"], "limit": 20, "cursor": "..." } Then response matches normal list response. But if you already have GET with query params, only add this if you truly need it.
This is helpful. Another design point: should I use nouns like “posts” or “articles”? The domain is basically posts.

Use domain language that matches what the resource is. If your API is for “posts”, call them posts. “Articles” implies longer-form content; “entries” is generic. Consistency is key: resource names should be stable because changing them later is breaking.
For URL path segments, do you prefer kebab-case or snake_case?

Kebab-case is common in URLs: - /user-settings Snake_case is also used: - /user_settings Pick one and keep consistent. Many prefer kebab-case for readability and because underscores can be less visible in some contexts. Plural nouns with kebab-case is a good default: - /api/v1/user-profiles (if needed)
If I have /users and a profile subresource, would it be /users/{id}/profile or /users/{id}/user-profile?
/users/{id}/profile is simpler. The parent already indicates user context, so “user-profile” is redundant.
For audit logs, should I have /audit-logs or /users/{id}/audit-logs?

Depends on scope: - If audit logs are global and filterable: /audit-logs?user_id=... - If primarily accessed per user: /users/{id}/audit-logs (alias) Audit logs can get large; consider pagination and retention. Also consider permissions: audit logs are often sensitive, so you might restrict fields or access.
For sensitive endpoints, do you ever use POST instead of GET to avoid query params showing up in logs?

Some do, but it’s not a strong security measure by itself; bodies can also be logged. Better approaches: - Avoid putting secrets in URLs at all. - Use POST with body for large/complex filters if needed, but not purely for “security through obscurity.” - Control logging and redaction at the server/proxy level. For typical filters like user_id, it’s fine in query params. For truly sensitive data, don’t send it as a parameter.
Makes sense. For endpoints returning lists, should I use “items” or “data” as the array key?

Both are common. “data” is generic; “items” is descriptive for lists. If you wrap lists and not singletons, “items” might be clearer: { "items": [...], "paging": ... } But “data” is widely used and works for both. Pick one and stick with it.
If I pick “items”, should single resource responses be { "item": {...} }?

You can, but it’s not necessary. Many APIs only wrap lists. If you want symmetrical wrappers: - list: { items: [...] } - single: { item: {...} } That’s consistent but adds verbosity. Either approach is fine.
I think I’ll do list wrapper with items, and single returns raw object. That should be okay.
Yes. Then your list response might be: { "items": [ ... ], "paging": { "next_cursor": "...", "has_more": true } } And single: { ... } Just ensure error shape is consistent.
For error codes, do you prefer numeric codes or string codes?

String codes are better: - Stable and descriptive: "invalid_request", "not_found", "rate_limited" Numeric codes are redundant with HTTP status and less readable. Also consider namespacing codes: - "posts.title_too_long" But that can be too specific. A middle ground: general codes plus field details.
If I have localized error messages, should message be localized or always English?

If you plan localization: - Keep error.code stable and language-agnostic. - message can be localized based on Accept-Language, but it complicates caching and tests. If you don’t need localization now, keep messages in English and rely on error.code for programmatic handling. You can add localization later without breaking if clients primarily use code.
For request bodies, should I accept unknown fields or reject them?

Prefer rejecting unknown fields (400/422). It catches typos and prevents clients from thinking something was saved when it wasn’t. If you want forward compatibility (clients sending fields from newer versions), that’s more relevant when you expect mixed versions. With /v1 and controlled clients, rejecting unknown fields is usually fine. Document it.
For PATCH, if a client sends an unknown field, should I reject the whole request or ignore that field?
Reject the whole request with a clear error. Ignoring can hide bugs. If you want to be permissive, do it consistently, but strict validation is usually better for API quality.
One last thing: should I include “self” URL in each resource representation?

Optional. It can be convenient, but it also couples representations to URL structure and base URL. If you do include it: - Use a relative path: "/api/v1/posts/p1" Or include an id and let clients build URLs. Most clients don’t need self links if your URL patterns are predictable. I’d skip unless you have a specific use.
Thanks. Circling back to the initial question, if I choose /posts?user_id=... as canonical, and /users/{id}/posts as alias, how do I ensure they don’t drift?

A few practical tactics: - Route both endpoints to the same handler/controller function. - Normalize inputs early: convert /users/{id}/posts into an internal query object { user_id: id } and pass through the same list logic. - Keep shared tests that hit both endpoints and assert identical responses (aside from maybe a self link). - In docs, clearly label one as “alias” or “shortcut”. Also ensure both support the same query params (status, limit, cursor, sort).
If I’m writing tests, what should I assert to confirm equivalence?

For the same underlying dataset and same effective query: - Status code equal. - Response schema equal. - items arrays equal (order and content). - paging fields equal (next_cursor, has_more). - Error behavior equal for invalid params. Be careful: if you include any “links” that contain the request URL, those might differ. If so, either omit request-specific links or normalize them in tests.
That’s a good point about links. I’ll probably omit them. Any final advice on endpoint structure pitfalls?

Common pitfalls: - Over-nesting and mixing styles inconsistently. - Inconsistent pagination/sorting across endpoints. - Ambiguous naming (user vs author). - Returning different shapes for similar endpoints. - Not documenting defaults (sort order, limit). - Silent ignoring of invalid params/fields. If you keep resources shallow, use consistent query filtering, and standardize responses and errors, your API will feel cohesive.
Great. I’ll start with /api/v1/posts and /api/v1/users, use /posts?user_id=... as canonical, cursor pagination, and consistent error envelopes. If I have more questions later, I’ll ask.
Sounds like a solid plan. When you implement, pay special attention to sorting + cursor rules and to strict validation—those two areas prevent a lot of subtle client issues.
What's the difference between a Docker image and a container? I keep conflating them.

A Docker image is an immutable template (a snapshot) that contains a filesystem plus metadata (like default command, environment variables, exposed ports). A container is a running (or stopped) instance created from an image, with a writable layer on top and runtime state (processes, network, mounts). A mental model that usually helps: - Image = recipe + ingredients list + prepped pantry (read-only). - Container = the actual meal you cooked from that recipe (has state, can be changed, can be stopped/started). More concretely: - You build/pull an image: docker build / docker pull - You create/run a container from that image: docker run - Many containers can be created from the same image, each with its own state.
Okay, so when I run docker run ubuntu, am I “creating an image” or “creating a container”?

docker run ubuntu creates a container (and runs it). If the ubuntu image isn’t present locally, Docker will pull the ubuntu image first, then create a container from it, then start it. So the sequence is: 1) Ensure image exists locally (pull if needed) 2) Create container (allocate writable layer, set up config) 3) Start container (launch process inside) If you want to see this separation: - docker pull ubuntu (just get image) - docker create ubuntu (create container but don’t start) - docker start <container_id> (start it)
What does “writable layer” mean? Is that like a disk?

Sort of. Docker images are typically built as a stack of read-only layers (each layer coming from an instruction in a Dockerfile, like RUN, COPY). When you create a container, Docker adds one more layer on top: a thin writable layer called the container layer. Changes made while the container runs—creating files, modifying files, installing packages—go into that writable layer. The lower image layers remain unchanged. It’s not a “disk” in the traditional sense, but it behaves like a filesystem. Under the hood Docker uses a storage driver (overlay2 is common on Linux) that merges the read-only layers with the writable layer into one unified view.
So if I install something inside a running container, like apt-get install curl, it goes into the writable layer. If I delete the container, I lose it?

Exactly. If you install curl inside a container and then remove that container (docker rm), that writable layer is deleted, so those changes are gone. If you want curl to be part of the “template” so every future container has it, you’d bake it into an image by writing a Dockerfile and building an image. For persistent data you want to keep independently of a container’s lifecycle, you typically use volumes or bind mounts.
I’ve also heard about “committing” a container into an image. Is that legit or bad practice?

It’s legit as a feature (docker commit), but it’s generally discouraged for repeatable workflows. Pros: - Quick way to capture a container’s current filesystem state into an image. - Useful for debugging or one-off experiments. Cons: - Not reproducible: you don’t have a clear, version-controlled description of how the image was built. - Easy to accidentally include unwanted state (cache files, secrets, history). - Harder to review and maintain. Best practice for most cases: write a Dockerfile, build images from it, and use volumes for data.
Speaking of Dockerfiles, how do layers actually get created? Like each line is a layer?

Roughly: many Dockerfile instructions create a new layer, but not literally every line in a “visual” sense—each instruction is evaluated in order, and certain instructions (RUN, COPY, ADD) typically create a filesystem layer. Others (CMD, ENTRYPOINT, ENV, EXPOSE, WORKDIR, USER, etc.) create metadata changes, which are part of the image configuration; they still create a new image “step” and affect caching, but not always a big filesystem diff. Example: - RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl creates a layer with the changes to the filesystem (installed packages). - ENV FOO=bar changes image config. - CMD ["bash"] sets default command. The layering matters because Docker can cache steps: if earlier steps haven’t changed, it can reuse previously built layers.
Wait, caching confuses me. If I change one line in my Dockerfile, does everything rebuild?

Docker builds the Dockerfile from top to bottom. It can reuse cached results for each step if the inputs to that step are unchanged. Rules of thumb: - If you change a step, that step and all subsequent steps usually need rebuilding, because later steps depend on the filesystem state produced earlier. - If you change something earlier (like a COPY instruction), you invalidate cache for that step and everything after it. - If you change only a later step (like CMD), earlier layers remain cached. This is why Dockerfile ordering matters. You often put “less frequently changing” steps earlier (like installing dependencies) and “frequently changing” steps later (like copying application code).
Can you give a concrete example? Like with a simple Python app?

Sure. Imagine: 1) FROM python:3.12-slim 2) WORKDIR /app 3) COPY requirements.txt . 4) RUN pip install -r requirements.txt 5) COPY . . 6) CMD ["python", "main.py"] If you change main.py, then step 5 changes (COPY . .), so step 5 and 6 rerun, but step 4 can remain cached, so you don’t reinstall dependencies each build. If you change requirements.txt, then step 3 changes, which invalidates step 4 and everything after—so dependencies reinstall.
That makes sense. But what’s the difference between docker build and docker run again? I know build makes an image, run makes a container, but I keep mixing them when I’m typing commands.

docker build produces an image artifact (something you can list with docker images). It takes a build context (a directory of files) and a Dockerfile, and outputs an image tagged with a name:tag. docker run takes an image and creates+starts a container based on it. It’s a runtime operation. So: - Build time: docker build -t myapp:1.0 . - Run time: docker run --rm -p 8080:8080 myapp:1.0 Also: - docker build doesn’t execute your app in the final sense; it executes build steps (RUN instructions) to assemble the image. - docker run actually launches the container’s main process (the configured CMD/ENTRYPOINT, unless overridden).
What does --rm do?

--rm tells Docker to automatically remove the container when it exits. It’s handy for short-lived containers so you don’t accumulate stopped containers. Example: - docker run --rm ubuntu echo hello After it prints hello and exits, the container is removed. Without --rm, it would remain as a stopped container you could see with docker ps -a.
So containers can be “stopped” but still exist?

Yes. A container is an object with a filesystem (container writable layer), configuration, and metadata. When it’s “running,” it has active processes. When it’s “stopped,” the processes are gone, but the container object still exists and can be restarted (docker start) with its previous filesystem changes intact. So: - docker run creates and starts (new container) - docker stop stops a running container - docker start starts an existing stopped container - docker rm removes a container (stopped, unless you force)
If I run docker run twice with the same image, do I get two separate containers?

Exactly. Each docker run creates a new container by default. Two containers from the same image are independent: they each get their own writable layer, their own networking identity (IP), their own process tree, etc. You can see them with docker ps (running) or docker ps -a (all).
How do names work? Sometimes Docker assigns random names like “sleepy_morse.” Can I pick my own?

Yes. You can name a container with --name. Example: docker run --name my-ubuntu ubuntu sleep 1000 Then you can reference it by name: docker exec -it my-ubuntu bash docker logs my-ubuntu docker stop my-ubuntu If you don’t specify --name, Docker generates a fun random one.
docker exec is like “enter the container,” right?

It runs a new process inside an existing running container. It’s commonly used to get a shell. Example: docker exec -it my-ubuntu bash - -i keeps STDIN open - -t allocates a pseudo-TTY Note: docker exec requires the container to be running. If it’s stopped, you need to start it first.
What about docker attach? I’ve seen that too.

docker attach connects your terminal to the main process of the container (the one started by CMD/ENTRYPOINT). It’s different from exec, which starts a new process. attach is useful if you want to interact with the original process (like a REPL that’s the main process). But it can be awkward because signals and input go directly to that process, and if you detach incorrectly you might stop the container. exec is generally safer for “get a shell” tasks.
Images are immutable, you said. But when I tag an image with a new tag, am I changing the image?

Tagging doesn’t change the image content; it changes a name reference to an image ID (or more precisely, to a manifest/digest). Think of tags as pointers. An image has an ID (a hash-like value). You can assign multiple tags to the same image ID. Example: - myapp:latest and myapp:1.0 might point to the same image. If you rebuild and retag, myapp:latest might point to a different image.
So “latest” isn’t special?

It’s just a default tag name. If you don’t specify a tag, Docker assumes :latest. But it doesn’t automatically mean “most recent build” unless you manage it that way. It’s common to use latest as a moving pointer, but that can be confusing in deployments because “latest” can change.
I’m trying to understand registries too. When I do docker pull, where is it pulling from?

By default it pulls from Docker Hub (registry-1.docker.io), unless your image name specifies another registry. Image naming patterns: - ubuntu -> docker.io/library/ubuntu - nginx:1.27 -> docker.io/library/nginx:1.27 - username/myimage -> docker.io/username/myimage - ghcr.io/user/repo:tag -> GitHub Container Registry - registry.example.com/team/app:tag -> a custom registry So docker pull ubuntu pulls from Docker Hub’s official library.
And docker push uploads my image to a registry?

Yes, if you’re logged in (docker login) and have permission. You push a tagged image name that includes your namespace (or a registry path). Typical flow: 1) docker build -t username/myapp:1.0 . 2) docker push username/myapp:1.0 You can also tag an existing local image: docker tag myapp:1.0 username/myapp:1.0 then push.
I keep hearing “image digest” and “tag.” Are they different?

Yes: - Tag: a human-friendly mutable label like myapp:1.0 or myapp:latest. It can be moved to point to a different image over time. - Digest: a content-addressed immutable identifier like sha256:... that uniquely identifies the image manifest/content. You can pull by digest: docker pull ubuntu@sha256:... That guarantees you get exactly that content, regardless of how tags change.
When I run a container, is it always using the exact image I have locally?

It uses the local image referenced by the tag at the time you run it. It won’t automatically check the registry for updates unless you pull manually (or use docker run --pull=always in newer Docker versions). So if you have nginx:latest locally, docker run nginx:latest uses that local one. If someone updated nginx:latest in the registry, you won’t get it unless you docker pull nginx:latest (or use --pull=always).
Oh, that explains why I sometimes don’t see updates. What is --pull=always exactly?

It tells docker run to always attempt to pull the image before creating the container. Behavior: - If a newer image exists in the registry for that tag, it pulls it. - If the image isn’t present locally, it pulls it (like usual). - If the pull fails, behavior can depend on Docker version/settings, but generally it errors. Example: docker run --pull=always nginx:latest
Now, containers vs VMs: are containers just lightweight VMs?

They’re lighter than VMs but not the same thing. VM: - Includes a full guest OS kernel. - Hardware virtualization through a hypervisor. - Stronger isolation boundary typically. Container: - Shares the host kernel (on Linux). - Isolated using kernel features: namespaces (process, network, mount, etc.) and cgroups (resource limits). - Filesystem isolation via layered filesystem. On macOS/Windows, Docker runs Linux containers inside a lightweight VM because the host kernel isn’t Linux. But conceptually, containers are OS-level virtualization, not hardware virtualization.
You mentioned namespaces and cgroups. Can you explain briefly what those do?

Namespaces isolate what a process can “see”: - pid namespace: process IDs (container has its own PID 1, etc.) - net namespace: network interfaces, IP addresses, ports - mnt namespace: mount points/filesystem view - uts namespace: hostname - ipc namespace: shared memory, semaphores - user namespace: user/group IDs mapping (optional) cgroups (control groups) limit and account for resources: - CPU shares/quotas - memory limits - block I/O - pids limit (number of processes) Docker uses these to make containers feel like separate machines while sharing the same kernel.
Is a container always one process? People say “one process per container.”

Docker doesn’t enforce one process per container; it’s a guideline. A container can run multiple processes, but Docker’s design is centered around one main process as PID 1. Why the guideline exists: - Simpler lifecycle: the container’s health corresponds to that process. - Easier logging and monitoring. - Easier scaling: run multiple containers rather than multiple services in one container. But there are legit cases for multiple processes (using a supervisor, sidecars in orchestration contexts, etc.). Still, for learning, “one main process” is a good default.
PID 1 has special behavior, right? I saw something about signals.

Yes. In Linux, PID 1 is special: it has different default signal-handling behavior and is responsible for reaping zombie processes (child processes that exited but haven’t been waited on). In containers: - If your app is PID 1 and it doesn’t handle SIGTERM properly, docker stop might not gracefully stop it. - If it spawns child processes and doesn’t reap them, zombies can accumulate. Solutions: - Ensure your app handles signals. - Use an init process like tini (Docker has --init option) to handle reaping and forwarding signals. Example: docker run --init ...
Good to know. Back to images: what is a “base image”?

A base image is the starting point in a Dockerfile’s FROM line. It provides an initial filesystem and sometimes runtime environment. Examples: - FROM ubuntu:24.04 (general OS base) - FROM python:3.12-slim (includes Python installed) - FROM node:22-alpine (Node.js on Alpine Linux) - FROM scratch (empty base, for statically linked binaries) Choosing a base image affects size, security, and convenience.
Is Alpine always better because it’s smaller?

Not always. Alpine is small because it uses musl libc and BusyBox, but that can cause compatibility issues for some software expecting glibc. Also, “smaller” isn’t the only metric—security updates, available packages, performance, and debugging tools matter. For many apps, Debian/Ubuntu slim images are a good balance. For Go apps with static binaries, scratch or distroless can be great.
Distroless? What’s that?

Distroless images contain only the runtime dependencies needed to run your app, not a full Linux distribution with package managers and shells. The goal is smaller attack surface and fewer unnecessary binaries. Tradeoff: harder to debug interactively because there’s no shell inside. You typically debug with logs, or by using a separate debug image.
So if I use distroless, I can’t docker exec bash?

Typically no, because there is no bash (and sometimes no shell at all). You can still exec your app’s binary or other included tools, but usually it’s minimal. A common pattern: - Use a normal base image for building and debugging. - Use distroless for final runtime stage via multi-stage builds.
Multi-stage builds seem powerful. How do they relate to images and containers?

Multi-stage builds are a Dockerfile feature that lets you build multiple intermediate images (“stages”) and copy artifacts from one stage to another, producing a final image that contains only what you need. This is still image-building. It doesn’t directly create containers unless you run them. Example pattern: - Stage 1: build (has compilers, dependencies) - Stage 2: runtime (only the compiled output) So you might compile a binary in stage 1 and then COPY it into a slim stage 2.
Could you show a simple multi-stage example? Maybe for a Go app?

Example: FROM golang:1.22 AS build WORKDIR /src COPY go.mod go.sum ./ RUN go mod download COPY . . RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -o /out/app ./cmd/app FROM scratch COPY --from=build /out/app /app ENTRYPOINT ["/app"] The final image is tiny because it includes only the statically linked binary.
That’s neat. If I run a container from that scratch-based image, does it still have a filesystem?

Yes, but it’s minimal. Even scratch images still have a filesystem layer (the one you add via COPY). It just doesn’t include typical OS files. Your binary might not have things like CA certificates unless you include them. That’s a common gotcha: HTTPS requests may fail because there’s no cert bundle.
Ah, so for HTTPS I might need CA certs. How would I include them?

One approach is to use a small base that includes certs (like gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12 which includes CA certs in some variants), or copy certs from a builder image: Example snippet: - In build stage (Debian-based), certs may be at /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt - In final stage, copy that file into the same path. But the exact path depends on distro. Distroless often handles this for you.
Switching gears: volumes confuse me. Are volumes part of the image or the container?

Volumes are separate from both image and container. They’re managed storage that lives outside the container’s writable layer. Key points: - Image: read-only template. - Container writable layer: ephemeral changes tied to container lifecycle. - Volume: persistent data storage managed by Docker, can outlive containers. When you mount a volume into a container, it overlays a directory in the container filesystem.
What’s the difference between a volume and a bind mount?

Bind mount: maps a specific host path into the container. Example: docker run -v /home/me/data:/data ubuntu Volume: Docker-managed storage with a name, stored in Docker’s area. Example: docker volume create mydata docker run -v mydata:/data ubuntu Differences: - Bind mounts are great for local development (edit code on host, container sees changes). - Volumes are better for persistent app data without coupling to a specific host path, and they’re easier to back up/migrate in Docker terms.
When I mount something, does it hide what’s already in that directory inside the image?

Yes. If you mount a volume or bind mount to /app, whatever was in /app in the image becomes hidden (not deleted, just not visible) while the mount is active. This can surprise people: - You COPY files into /app in the image. - Then you run with -v $(pwd):/app - Now you see the host directory contents instead, not the image’s /app.
I think I ran into that. My container couldn’t find a file that I swear I copied. That explains it.
Exactly. In dev, that’s often intentional (use host code). In production, you typically don’t bind mount your app code; you bake it into the image.
Another thing: networking. If I run a web server in a container, why can’t I access it from my browser unless I do -p?

By default, containers run in an isolated network namespace. The service might be listening on port 80 inside the container, but that port isn’t exposed on the host. -p publishes a container port to the host: docker run -p 8080:80 nginx This maps host port 8080 to container port 80. Without -p, the container can still access the network outward, and other containers on the same Docker network can reach it, but the host won’t have a published port to connect to.
So EXPOSE in Dockerfile doesn’t actually publish the port?

Right. EXPOSE is documentation/metadata indicating the container listens on certain ports. It can be used by tooling, but it doesn’t open ports on the host by itself. You still need: - docker run -p host:container or - docker run -P (publish all exposed ports to random host ports)
What is -P used for?

-P publishes all exposed ports to random available host ports. For example, if an image exposes 80, Docker might map it to something like host port 32768. You can see the mapping with docker ps. It’s handy for quick tests but less predictable than specifying ports with -p.
How do I see what ports are mapped?
docker ps shows PORTS column. Or: docker port <container_name> Example: docker port mynginx
Cool. Back to the image/container difference: where are images stored on my machine? And containers?

Docker stores both in its internal storage area, not in your project directory (unless you use bind mounts). The exact location depends on OS and Docker setup. On Linux, Docker data is often under /var/lib/docker (requires root to access). There you’ll see: - image layers - container writable layers - volumes - metadata On macOS/Windows, Docker Desktop uses a VM, and the data is inside that VM’s disk image. You usually interact via Docker commands: - docker images - docker ps -a - docker system df (disk usage) - docker volume ls
If containers have writable layers, do stopped containers take up disk space?

Yes. Stopped containers still have their writable layers and metadata, so they consume disk space. That’s why periodically cleaning up helps. Commands: - docker container prune (remove stopped containers) - docker image prune (remove unused images) - docker volume prune (remove unused volumes) - docker system prune (remove unused containers, networks, images; add --volumes to include volumes) Be cautious with prune commands—make sure you won’t delete something you care about.
“Unused images” means what exactly?

Typically “dangling” images are those not tagged and not referenced by any container. But Docker also has the concept of “unused” images not referenced by any container (even if tagged). - docker image prune removes dangling images by default. - docker image prune -a removes all images not used by at least one container. So if you built an image but never created a container from it, it might be pruned with -a.
If I run a container with --rm, does that mean it won’t keep its writable layer after exit?
Correct. --rm removes the container at exit, so its writable layer goes away. Any data not in a volume/bind mount is lost.
Let’s say I want to keep the container around but reset it to the original image state. Is there a “reset” command?

Not exactly. The typical way is: - Remove the container (docker rm) - Recreate it from the image (docker run again) If you want persistent data, put that data in a volume; then you can recreate containers freely without losing the data.
That makes sense. So the image is like the baseline, and containers are disposable.
Exactly. That’s a core container philosophy: treat containers as ephemeral. Build immutable images; run disposable containers; persist data externally.
But what about database containers? People run Postgres in Docker all the time. Aren’t those not disposable?

The container is disposable; the data is not. When people run Postgres in Docker, they generally mount a volume for /var/lib/postgresql/data. Then you can delete and recreate the Postgres container without losing the database, because the data lives in the volume. Example: docker run --name pg -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=pass -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data -p 5432:5432 postgres
If I don’t use a volume for Postgres, it stores data in the container layer and will be lost when I remove the container?
Yes. It would be stored in the container’s writable layer. If you remove the container, the database files are gone.
I think I understand. But I still get confused when I see “IMAGE” column in docker ps output. That’s the image the container was created from?

Yes. docker ps shows running containers with the image reference used to create them. It may show the tag name (like nginx:latest) that was used at creation time. Even if the tag later points to a different image after an update, the container still uses the image it was created from (image ID). The display might still show the tag, but the container is tied to the specific image ID.
How can I see the image ID a container is using?
Use docker inspect. Example: docker inspect --format='{{.Image}}' <container> That prints the image ID (a SHA-like identifier). Or: docker inspect <container> and look for "Image": "...".
Is there a way to see what layers an image has?

Yes: - docker history <image> shows the layers/steps and their sizes and commands (not perfect but helpful). - docker inspect <image> can show rootfs layers. For even more detail, tools like dive can visualize layers, but sticking to Docker CLI: docker history myapp:1.0
docker history shows “<missing>” sometimes. Why?

That can happen because of how images are built/stored and because intermediate layers may not have local IDs in the same way, especially with newer build backends (BuildKit) and multi-stage builds. The history still gives you a sense of what each layer corresponds to.
BuildKit is another term I keep hearing. What is it?

BuildKit is Docker’s newer build engine that improves performance, caching, and features (like parallel build steps, better caching, secrets mounts, SSH forwarding). It’s often enabled by default now. You might notice BuildKit output looks different (more structured). It can also do things like: - cache mounts for package managers - secret mounts so secrets aren’t baked into layers
Secrets mounts sound important. How do secrets get accidentally baked into images?

If you do something like: RUN echo "mysecret" > /app/secret.txt or COPY . . and your build context includes a .env file with secrets, those can end up in an image layer. Even if you delete them in a later layer, the secret might still exist in the earlier layer history, because layers are additive. That’s why you must avoid putting secrets in build context or layers. Use: - .dockerignore to exclude sensitive files - BuildKit secrets to supply secrets at build time without persisting them in layers - Environment variables at runtime (with care), or external secret managers in more advanced setups
Wait, if I delete a file in a later layer, it still exists in earlier layers?

Yes. Layers are like diffs. If a file is added in one layer and removed in a later layer, the final merged filesystem view doesn’t show it, but the data may still be present in the image layers. Anyone with access to the image could potentially extract layers. So: never add secrets in any layer at all.
That’s kind of scary. So .dockerignore is important.

Very. .dockerignore controls what files are sent as build context to the Docker daemon. If you run docker build ., Docker sends the directory contents (minus ignored files). If secrets are in that context, they can be copied into the image by mistake or even just increase build time. Typical .dockerignore entries: - .git - node_modules - __pycache__ - *.env - dist/build artifacts (depending on workflow)
How does the build context work exactly? Is Docker building locally or sending files somewhere?

Docker build uses a “build context” directory. Historically, the Docker CLI would send that context (tar archive) to the Docker daemon, which might be local or remote. With Docker Desktop, there’s still a daemon inside the VM, so context is transferred. BuildKit can optimize this, but conceptually: - The Dockerfile can only reference files inside the build context (for COPY/ADD). - Big context = slower builds. So you want a minimal context and a good .dockerignore.
If I have a Dockerfile in a subdirectory, can I still use the root directory as context?
Yes. You can specify Dockerfile path with -f and context separately. Example: docker build -f docker/Dockerfile -t myapp:dev . Here context is ., Dockerfile is docker/Dockerfile.
I think I’ve seen COPY fail because it couldn’t find a file. That might be because it wasn’t in the context.
Exactly. COPY only sees files in the build context. If you try to COPY ../something outside context, it won’t work (and Docker prevents it for security reasons).
Coming back to containers: when I run docker run -it ubuntu bash, it opens a shell. When I exit bash, the container stops. Is that because bash was the main process?

Yes. In that case, bash is PID 1 (the main process). When you exit bash, PID 1 exits, so the container stops. If you want a container to keep running, the main process must keep running (like a server, or sleep).
So people sometimes do sleep infinity to keep it alive?
Yes, for debugging. Example: docker run -d --name test ubuntu sleep infinity Then you can exec into it to poke around: docker exec -it test bash
What does -d do?
-d runs the container in detached mode (in the background). Docker prints the container ID and returns you to the terminal.
How do logs work? If I run a server in a container, where do logs go?

Docker captures stdout/stderr of the container’s main process and stores it via a logging driver (default is usually json-file). You can view them with: docker logs <container> Best practice: applications should log to stdout/stderr rather than writing log files inside the container (unless you mount a volume for logs). That way logs are handled by Docker and can be collected by external systems.
If my app writes log files in the container filesystem, they get lost when the container is removed?
Yes, unless you mount a volume/bind mount. That’s why stdout logging is encouraged.
What about environment variables. Are they part of the image or container?

Both can set them, but in different ways: - Image can define default ENV values in the Dockerfile (ENV KEY=value). - Container can set/override env vars at runtime with docker run -e KEY=value. At runtime, the container environment is the final merged result.
If I set ENV in Dockerfile, can I override it when running?
Yes. Runtime -e overrides Dockerfile ENV. Example: Dockerfile: ENV MODE=prod Run: docker run -e MODE=dev myimage Inside container, MODE=dev.
I’m also confused about CMD vs ENTRYPOINT. How does that affect containers?

CMD and ENTRYPOINT define what runs when a container starts. - ENTRYPOINT: defines the executable (the “main” command). - CMD: provides default arguments to ENTRYPOINT, or if no ENTRYPOINT, CMD is the command. Common patterns: 1) ENTRYPOINT ["python", "app.py"] CMD ["--port", "8080"] Then docker run image uses python app.py --port 8080 by default, but you can override CMD args: docker run image --port 9090 2) CMD ["bash"] No entrypoint; default command is bash; docker run image overrides it if you supply a command. Misunderstandings here often cause “why doesn’t my container run” issues.
If I run docker run myimage echo hi, what happens?

It depends on ENTRYPOINT/CMD. - If image has no ENTRYPOINT, and CMD is something, then providing echo hi replaces CMD, so it runs echo hi. - If image has ENTRYPOINT set, and CMD provides default args, then echo hi is treated as arguments to ENTRYPOINT (unless you use --entrypoint to override). You can inspect with: docker image inspect myimage Look at Config.Entrypoint and Config.Cmd.
Is there a way to override ENTRYPOINT at runtime?
Yes: docker run --entrypoint /bin/sh myimage -c 'echo hi' (assuming /bin/sh exists in the image) This replaces the image’s ENTRYPOINT for that run.
I think I’m getting it. But sometimes I see “OCI runtime error” and it says “exec: ‘bash’: executable file not found.” That’s because the image doesn’t have bash?
Exactly. Many minimal images (alpine has /bin/sh but not bash by default; distroless has neither) won’t have bash. Use sh, or install bash, or choose a base that includes it.
How do I check what’s inside an image if it doesn’t have a shell?

Options: - Use docker export on a container and inspect filesystem outside (a bit clunky). - Use a debug variant image. - Temporarily build a debug image with a shell. - Use tools like “docker run --rm -it --entrypoint” only if there is some shell. - Use “busybox” or “alpine” as a helper container and mount the target image’s filesystem? That’s not straightforward because images aren’t directly mountable via Docker CLI, but you can create a container from the image and then use docker cp to copy files out. A practical approach: 1) docker create --name tmp myimage 2) docker cp tmp:/path/in/container ./localcopy 3) docker rm tmp
docker cp works on stopped containers?
Yes, it can work on stopped containers because it’s copying from the container’s filesystem snapshot.
Nice. Another thing: When I build an image, does Docker create containers during the build?

During build, Docker runs build steps in intermediate containers (conceptually) to produce layers. With BuildKit, it’s more advanced, but the idea remains: each RUN instruction executes in an isolated environment and the result is committed as a layer. Those intermediate containers are typically not visible in docker ps after the build (they’re cleaned up). So you don’t manually manage them, but yes, container-like execution happens during build.
That explains why RUN commands can access the internet and install packages. They’re executed in that intermediate environment.
Exactly.
If images are immutable, how do updates work? Like security updates in base image.

You rebuild your image against a newer base image version. Example: - Your Dockerfile uses FROM python:3.12-slim - If python:3.12-slim gets updated with security patches, you need to rebuild your image to incorporate those changes. If you pin to a digest, you’ll need to update that digest explicitly to get updates.
Should I pin tags or digests?

Tradeoff: - Pinning digests gives reproducibility (same image every time) but you must manage updates manually. - Pinning version tags (like python:3.12.4-slim) is a middle ground: mostly stable, but still a tag. - Using floating tags (python:3.12-slim or latest) is easiest but less reproducible. Many people pin major/minor versions and rebuild regularly, scanning for vulnerabilities.
Vulnerability scanning is a whole other thing. But I’ll keep it in mind.
Good plan. Even without scanning tools, regularly rebuilding helps pick up patched base layers.
Can two images share layers?

Yes. That’s one reason images are efficient. If two images use the same base layers, Docker stores them once and references them. Example: - myapp and anotherapp both FROM python:3.12-slim. They’ll share the python base layers. Only app-specific layers differ.
And containers can share image layers too?
Yes. Multiple containers from the same image share the image’s read-only layers. Each container has its own writable layer on top.
So disk usage is mostly the writable layers plus unique layers.
Exactly.
I’m thinking about performance: does the layered filesystem slow things down?

There can be overhead, especially for heavy write workloads because of copy-on-write behavior. Reading is usually fine, but writing to files that exist in lower layers can trigger “copy up” (copying file into writable layer). For databases or heavy write workloads, volumes often perform better and are recommended.
Copy-on-write means if I modify a file that’s in the image, it gets copied into the container layer first?

Yes. With overlay filesystems, when you modify a file that exists in a lower read-only layer, the filesystem copies it up into the writable layer, then modifies the copy. That preserves immutability of the lower layers.
That’s clever. Does that mean if I create a new file, it just goes into writable layer without copying?
Exactly. New files go directly into the writable layer.
I’m trying to connect this with “docker diff”. What does docker diff do?

docker diff <container> shows filesystem changes in the container compared to the image it was created from. It lists: - A: added files - C: changed files - D: deleted files It’s helpful to see what changed in the writable layer.
Nice. So if I install curl in a container, docker diff would show new files under /usr/bin and so on.
Yes, it would show lots of changes.
Is that how docker commit works—turning that diff into an image layer?

Pretty much. docker commit takes the container’s writable layer changes and creates a new image layer on top of the original image, plus metadata changes you specify (like a new CMD via -c). It’s essentially “snapshot current container state as a new image.”
If I commit, does it include running processes?
No. It captures filesystem state and some metadata, not runtime state like memory or process tree. When you run a container from the committed image, processes start fresh.
Got it. Another question: What’s the difference between docker create and docker run? You mentioned it earlier.

docker create creates a container object from an image but does not start it. docker run is basically docker create + docker start (plus it can pull, set options, attach terminal, etc.). So: - docker create ubuntu outputs container ID (stopped) - docker start <id> starts it
When would I use docker create?

It’s useful if you want to: - Create a container with a bunch of options but start it later. - Create a container to copy files out (docker cp) without running it. - Set up a container and then start it with docker start -a to attach. But most of the time people just use docker run.
How do I list images and containers again?
Images: - docker images or docker image ls Containers: - docker ps (running) - docker ps -a (all)
If I remove an image, what happens to containers created from it?

You generally can’t remove an image if any containers (even stopped) reference it, unless you force remove. Docker will warn that it’s in use. If you force remove, you can break those containers because they rely on the image layers. Docker tries to prevent that.
So removing containers first is typical.
Yes: 1) docker rm <containers> 2) docker rmi <images>
I saw “dangling images” after builds. Why does that happen?

Dangling images often appear when you rebuild an image tag. The old image that used to have the tag loses its tag and becomes dangling (untagged) if nothing references it. Example: - Build myapp:latest -> image ID abc - Rebuild myapp:latest -> new image ID def Now abc might have no tags; it’s dangling unless a container references it. You can clean dangling images with docker image prune.
That makes sense. Are those dangling images still taking up space?
Yes, until pruned, unless their layers are shared and still used elsewhere. But the metadata and any unique layers still consume space.
I think I’ve mostly got images vs containers. But can we do a quick recap in a super concrete way?

Sure: - Image: a read-only blueprint. Actions: build (docker build), pull (docker pull), tag (docker tag), push (docker push), list (docker images). Stored as layers; immutable content. - Container: a runnable instance of an image. Actions: run (docker run), start/stop (docker start/stop), exec (docker exec), logs (docker logs), remove (docker rm), list (docker ps). Has a writable layer and runtime state. - Data persistence: use volumes/bind mounts, not container layer.
Great. Now I want to actually practice. Suppose I have a tiny app: a Python script that prints “hello” and exits. How would I containerize it properly?

Create: 1) main.py: print("hello") 2) Dockerfile: FROM python:3.12-slim WORKDIR /app COPY main.py . CMD ["python", "main.py"] Build: docker build -t hello-python:1.0 . Run: docker run --rm hello-python:1.0 You’ll see “hello” printed, then the container exits and is removed.
If I run it without --rm, it’ll create a stopped container afterward?
Yes. You’d see it in docker ps -a.
What if I want to mount my local file so I don’t rebuild every time?

Use a bind mount: docker run --rm -v "$PWD":/app -w /app python:3.12-slim python main.py Here you’re not even using your custom image; you’re using python base image and mounting your code. That’s often fine for quick iteration. If you want to use your image but still mount: docker run --rm -v "$PWD":/app hello-python:1.0 But remember: it will hide whatever is in /app in the image.
That’s a good point. If my image expects dependencies installed, mounting might hide them?

Dependencies installed via pip in the image usually go into site-packages elsewhere, not /app, so it might be fine. But if you copy your code into /app and then mount /app, you’re overriding the code directory, which is usually intended in dev.
For a bigger app, I’d probably use requirements.txt and install dependencies.
Right, and you’d structure Dockerfile to cache dependency installs: COPY requirements.txt . RUN pip install -r requirements.txt COPY . .
One last confusion: sometimes people say “container image” as if it’s one thing.

Yeah, “container image” is just the full term for “image used to run containers.” People often shorten it to “image.” The key is remembering that the image is the static artifact, and the container is the running (or stopped) instance.
